title: Fixed-Head Disk Reader for the CDC 3300
description: The fixed-head disk reader for the timing disk on the CDC 3300. The CDC3600 was the first computer in Control Data Corporation's 3000 line, introduced in June 1963. It was designed for scientific computing applications, used a 48-bit word size, and could execute 1 million instructions per second (1 MIP). The 3000 series had both 24-bit (compatible with the lower-3000 series machines, packed 2 per word in the 3600's case) and 48-bit instructions; all models used core memory and and one's complement arithmetic. The CDC3000 was a popular series, sustaining Control Data for the duration of the development of the more powerful CDC6000 series. The 3000 series' adopters included the IRS and several Eastern Bloc countries, among others. Additionally, Oregon State University maintained a CDC3300 through approximately 1980, running an independently developed operating system called OS3 (Oregon State Open Shop Operating System).

title: Walker Textbook Diskettes and Flyer
description: Henry M. Walker published "Introduction to Computing and Computer Science with Pascal" with Little, Brown and Company in 1986. The book covered material for an introductory full-year course in computer science. As was common at the time, a flyer promoted the book to instructors, highlighting chapter and section titles and providing additional context materials were sent to the publisher on 5-1/4" floppy diskettes. The diskettes here contain programs, exercises, and the text itself.

title: TeleVideo TVI-912 ASCII terminal
description: North Korean-born Dr. Kyupin Philip Hwang, a Utah State University graduate, started a company in 1975 which sold CRT monitors for arcade games. As this project matured, Hwang went on in 1979 to found TeleVideo Systems, Inc., which manufactured a series of text terminals and later, CP/M and MS-DOS compatible personal computers, which notably could be connected in networks as large as sixteen machines via serial interfaces using a proprietary protocol. Hwang headquartered TeleVideo in San Jose, California. The TVI-912, introduced in 1979, the same year TeleVideo was founded, was one of TeleVideo's earliest terminals, preceded only by the TVI-910. It supports a 96-character ASCII upper and lower case alphabet, displays 24 80-character lines on screen, and weighs in at 30 lbs. It supports 9 data rates on RS232 serial interfaces, ranging from 75 to 9600 baud (bits/second) with half-duplex and full-duplex conversation and block transfer modes. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Televideo http://vt100.net/televideo/ http://maben.homeip.net/static/S100/televideo/termnals/ (some cool TVI-925 schematics on this site... might be worth including, even though it's a different model) manual, available on vt100.net, saved at /home/cooperth/museum/items/TeleVideo TVI-912/912b-om.pdf

title: Vacuum Tube Decade Counter
description: From Stone's notes: Decade counter - 30 pins in top; every third one represents a digit. To increment the count, do successive lowering and raising volting on the pins adjacent to the digit pins: n n+1 * o o * low high low high low high Head of tube projects through numbered bezel - lighted dot identifies digit.

Nixie tubes
description: Nixie tubes are vacuum-sealed tubes that contain a wire mesh anode and a series of cathodes in the shape of members of a set of characters, typically arabic numerals. They are an example of code-cathode tubes, filled with a mixture of gases usually containing neon at a low pressure. Applying around 170V DC between a cathode and the anode will cause the cathode to emit a glow, creating the effect of displaying the corresponding character. They were introduced in 1954 by Burroughs Corporation, and used in clocks, frequency counters, early computers, and other electronic equipment. Manufacturing of nixies petered out by the early 1990s, but since the late 1990s there has been a nixie revival of sorts among electronics enthusiasts, largely because of their unique aesthetic. 8 total. It appears that several of these aren't actually nixie tubes, since they don't have any character-shaped cathodes, although the pin count, shape, and size of these mystery tubes are the same. They also have 9 pins, which Prof. Stone described as a "mystery" in his notes. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nixie_tube

PDP to VAX Kermit file copy instructions
description: Kermit, developed at Columbia University in 1981, is a protocol for file transfer, terminal emulation, character-set conversion, and other platform-independent communication services, known especially as a tool for standardized and efficient discourse between dissimilar systems. It was most popular in the 1980s, although current implementations such as C-Kermit (homepage linked below) are maintained. Kermit was originally developed for and primarily used to transmit data via serial connections, although some later implementations also run over networks. It was developed for everyday student use at Columbia to transfer files between mainframes and microcomputers with different character sets, and used for similar purposes at Grinnell, although it has been used in a wide range of applications, including some aboard the International Space Station. The depicted set of instructions document the process of using Kermit to transfer a file between PDP and VAX minicomputers the College operated concurrently circa 1985 by way of an intermediary Rainbow workstation. This awkward and involved process is an example of the kind of problem the MathLAN, which was still in early planning stages at this point, was designed to solve. References: Notes from a conversation with John Stone http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kermit_(protocol) http://www.columbia.edu/kermit/ck80.html

title: Osborne 1 luggable microcomputer
description: David Coahran received this in 1992. To quote Wikipedia, "The Osborne 1 was the first commercially successful portable microcomputer." It was released by Osborne Computer Corporation in April, 1981 as their flagship product. Osborne Computer initially enjoyed strong sales on it (competing products from Kaypro, Apple, IBM, and Compaq reduced Osborne Computer's market share) and started development on successor products, the Osborne Executive and Osborne Vixen. There was some speculation that Osborne Computer's bankruptcy in 1985 was a result of statements made by the founder, Adam Osborne, in 1983 about the Executive and/or Vixen's advanced planned specifications and capabilities long before those models would be available for release; allegedly, these statements hurt demand for the existing Osborne 1 line and were the primary impetus for bankruptcy. Proponents of this theory coined the term "Osborne Effect" to describe it and similar situations. Specifications: The Osborne 1 uses an 8-bit 4 MHz Z80 CPU, has 65KB of memory, and a tiny 52-character monochrome CRT screen. Additionally, it has dual 5 1/4" floppy drives; most unit shipped with drives for single-sided floppies, but a dual density upgrade was available and appears to be installed in this unit. The peripheral interfaces consist of an IEEE-488 parallel(?) port, and an RS232 1200 or 300 baud serial port. It runs the CP/M 2.2 operating system, a popular choice for the time, and this unit came to us with the following software: Came with a variety of 5 1/4" floppies in two boxes: 2x Grafiks version 2.0 system and example disks Grafiks system and example double-density disk Chartech 2.0 disks 1 and 2 SuperCalc 2 disk DG/SYSTEMS Media Master (1984) disk XtraKey Universal, Side2, SWP.com disk "Reprint copy #1" disk Stardrive Graphic Express program and data disks 2x CP/M System disk CP/M Utility disk 3x CP/M Extended Utility disk "Wordstar/Mailmerge, Footnote, Epson Bas, MBASIC" disk Microsoft CBASIC/MBASIC disk Wordstar/Mailmerge disk 2x SuperCalc disk Footnote 2.2 disk NOTE: The included note said that the machine started blowing fuses when Dr. Coahran received it in 1992 and no longer worked, and when I found it there wasn't a fuse installed. I put some paperclips in the fuse... port, and now it boots up and works fine as far as I can tell. (Ted Cooper 06-16-2009) Serial No: 010132 Received 06-15-2007, originally from David and Stacia Moffett, geologists at WSU (Washington State University). Originally purchased for personal use. References: Stone's notes from meeting with David Coahran upon receipt http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Osborne_0

DEC Rainbow 100 floppies used by Emily Moore
description: Notes from Stone: Professor Emily Moore used these floppy disks on the computer in her office between 1983 and 1987 -- a DEC Rainbow 100 with a monochrome, VT-220-like monitor. The Rainbow could boot either into CP/M or into an MS-DOS-like operating system. Much of her programming was done in Pascal or MATLAB, though interpreters for BASIC and Logo are also here. MASS-11 was a document-management system that GCCS (the predecessor of Grinnell ITS) advocated to faculty. Additional information: The 3 1/2" floppies were used on SunOS workstations, as opposed to the DEC Rainbow 100, although the MATLAB code would of course work on any platform with a MATLAB implementation. Notable among the MATLAB programs are visualization utilities Graph2D, Graph3D, solve, integrat, fseq (finite sequences), dirfield (direction fields for differential equations), fingraph (finite graphs). The programs were used in Calculus I and other math classes at Grinnell for a number of years, and were developed by Emily Moore and research students over two summers circa 1992. References: Notes provided by John Stone Notes from a conversation with John Stone

title: Discrete Logic/Memory Cards from the Nebula Machine
description: Notes from Steven Cunningham: The cards are hand-assembled and are from the Oregon State Nebula machine. The Nebula was built in 1965 by the Oregon State faculty and students; it was a one-of-a-kind machine. It had 34-bit words built on a glass delay line memory loop, with a total 4096-word memory. The original cost was $30,400 in parts and $16,600 in labor. It was decommissioned in the late 1970s and was being scrapped in 1980-81 when I was on sabbatical there. I latched onto as many of the parts as I could, but there were a number of people getting the best stuff and I was only a visitor (actually a graduate student again getting my MS in CS with an NSF SFPD grant).

The straight slide rules were not as accurate as the Atlas Slide Rule, which could get up to five figures, but were still quite good for school based work. title: Apple Macintosh SE FDHD
description: Released in August 1989, the Macintosh SE FDHD was an updated edition of the Macintosh SE (released March 1987) supporting 1.44MB High Density (HD) floppies. SE units came configured to support either a 20MB or 40MB hard drive or dual floppy drives. It appears that this unit contains a hard drive, or at least doesn't have dual floppy drives. Production of the Macintosh SE FDHD was discontinued in October 1990, when the Macintosh Classic came out. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macintosh_SE

title: Apple SilenType printer
description: Released in June 1979, the SilenType was the first printer from Apple Computer. It was essentially a rebranded Trendcom Model 200, but Apple replaced the digital control circuitry inside, removing a microprocessor and memory chips and offloading their duties to the attached computer. This reduced the cost of the printer substantially. It was compatible with the Apple II, Apple III, and Lisa(?) computers, and connected via a proprietary Apple SilenType port, requiring either a SilenType interface card or an Apple III with a built-in SilenType port. The SilenType was a thermal printer, supporting 80-column output and requiring special paper. Printouts were difficult to store because the paper was heat-sensitive and tended to fade over time. It was replaced by the Apple Dot Matrix Printer in October 1982. 2 of these in the museum: First one: Model No.: A2M0032 Serial No.: 030486 Evidently property of the University of Minnesota at some point, tagged with Property No.: 8200662 Second one: Model No.: A2M0032 Serial No.: 024235 References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_SilenType http://apple2history.org/history/ah13.html#06 (release date confirmation)

title: Apple Macintosh 128K
description: The Macintosh 128K was released in September 1984 in tandem with the Macintosh 512K. Although its specifications were equivalent to those of the original Macintosh (released in January 1984), its main logic board was essentially the Mac 512K's logic board minus the additional RAM. The Macintosh series was Apple's bid to bring the graphical user interface to middle-class personal computer users, and it enjoyed great success in this capacity. Many associate the original Macintosh with Ridley Scott's television commercial for it, "1984," which was aired only once during daytime television, in the third quarter of Super Bowl XVIII on January 22, 1984, two days before the Macintosh was first introduced. The ad references George Orwell's book of the same title, presenting a Big Brother character on a large screen (Steve Jobs has been quoted as suggesting that this is meant to represent IBM) and a woman with a Macintosh shirt who hurls a hammer at the screen, destroying it. To this day the advertisement is hailed as one of the greatest televised. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macintosh_128k http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1984_(advertisement)

title: Apple Macintosh IIcx
description: Released in March 1989, the Macintosh IIcx was a smaller, quieter, cheaper successor to the Macintosh IIx. Its internal parts also all snapped into place, without screws or other fasteners. Also, its case was the only one Apple ever designed to sit either horizontally or vertically. In general, the Macintosh II series was notable for including Color QuickDraw in ROM, allowing for 24-bit color (it was the first personal computer with this capability released) and much greater flexibility in what resolutions could be displayed and how many monitors could be connected. Model No.: M5650 Serial No.: F9328G1M5615 Included AppleColor High-Resolution RGB Monitor: Marked as copyrighted 1987, Apple Computer Model No.: M0401 Serial No.: 004751 References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macintosh_IIcx http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macintosh_II

title: IBM Personal Computer (PC) 5149
description: The IBM Personal Computer (PC) 5150 was introduced on August 12, 1981, and was powered by the Intel 8088 processing chip that ran at 4.77 MHz. The simplest configuration (costing about $1,565) had only 16K main memory and used a cassette as a storage medium. A more typical system (costing about $3,000) contained 64K main memory and two 5-1/4" floppy drives. (Hard disks were not available for the 5150.) IBM negotiated with Microsoft for the PC-DOS v1.0 operating system. This machine provides the origin of the term "PC-compatible." It was designed under the auspices of a project codenamed "Project Chess" managed by Don Estridge and consisting of just 12 people. The team was tasked with quickly designing (development took just a year) a product to compete with the Apple II and other popular small hobbyist computers. To accomplish this goal they chose to use a variety of pre-existing parts from third-party manufacturers and other IBM products, and to encourage widespread adoption and component/software development by third parties, the architecture was open, and circuit schematics, the BIOS source code, and other technical information were published. Interestingly a RISC processor, the IBM 801 (with a corresponding IBM operating system), was considered for inclusion in the PC's original design, but the Intel 8088 (which ran Microsoft DOS) had already been used in the IBM Datamaster microcomputer series and was for this reason easier to engineer and in general more available. Had Estridge's team chosen the IBM 801 instead of the Intel 8088, we might all have RISC processors today, and never have heard of a company called Microsoft. A variety of other manufacturers jumped on the open architecture and designed "IBM PC-compatible" microcomputers that worked with PC parts and software. The IBM PC itself also enjoyed great commercial success, largely thanks to partnerships with ComputerLand and Sears Roebuck for retail distribution. Model No.: 5150 Serial No.: 12856735150 References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_5150 http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_DOS

title: ROM from an Apple II
description: a set of four ROM for the Apple II computer; ROM (read only memory) contains permanent instructions for the computer

title: PROM Set
description: A set of six PROMs. PROM (programmable read-only memory)differentiates from ROM in that the PROM is programmed after it is constructed, as opposed to ROM which is programmed during its construction. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Programmable_read-only_memory

title: Silicon Wafers
description: Three silicon wafers from fairly early integrated circuit production; one is blank, one has memory chips, and one has processor chips.

title: CDC 6600 Core Memory
description: Known as magnetic core memory. Magnetic core memory is an early form of RAM (random access memory). This particular core comes from a CDC 6600, which is regarded as one of the first successful supercomputers. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Core_memory http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CDC_6600

title: Raytheon Core Memory
description: Known as magnetic core memory. Magnetic core memory is an early form of RAM (random access memory). This particular board was produced by Raytheon in 1971. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Core_memory http://www.computerhistory.org/collections/accession/X958.88

title: Portable Dot Matrix Printer
description: Dot matrix printers printed pages similar to a typewriter in that they had a print head that ran back and forth across the page. They differentiated from typewriters, because they created text with tiny dots, hence the name dot matrix. This particular printer was portable and worked with early Apple PowerBooks. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_matrix_printer

title: Hayes SMARTMODEM 1200
description: One of the first external modems, it was released in 1981. Smartmodem was the first modem that was able to exert complete control over a phone line. Hobbyists used this modem and cutting edge software to create the first bulletin board systems, which allowed users to upload and download software and data, and exchange messages. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hayes_Microcomputer_Products http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bulletin_board_system

title: UNIX Fire Extinguisher
description: A UNIX Fire Extinguisher, which is one of the two main brands of Fire Extinguishers in Spain. The irony here is that UNIX is also the name of a very influential operating system that was developed in 1969 by a few AT&T employees at Bell Labs. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix

title: Seagate ST-225 Hard Drive
description: This hard drive was developed by Seagate in 1984, and had a capacity of around 21.4MB. It was the single most common 20MB drive ever to be produced. References: http://redhill.net.au/d/d-a.html

title: FORTRAN Compiler
description: A FORTRAN compiler for an IBM 1130 as an object deck. A compiler program took the form of punched data (holes) in a deck of cards. The deck of cards was then placed before a programmer's code and then read in to the machine.

title: Power Tube
description: A device that was used to control circuitry. It is also known as a vacuum tube. It has since been largely replaced by the transistor. The transition from power tube to transistors occurred during the 1950's and 60's.

title: Electrostatic Memory
description: Electrostatic memory from the Oregon State Nebula machine (by Corning Glass Works; it contains a glass torus on which a charge was written, circulated, and read).

title: Paper Tape
description: Paper tape that was used for software distribution. The data is represented by the holes in the tape. The tape was read through a reader to enter data into the machine. The paper tape is the source code for a Tektronix graphics system, probably Plot-10 IGL.

title: IBM Cartridge Tape
description: An IBM data cartridge that was used for backups.

title: Two Burroughs SP 3200 Reel Tapes
description: Two magnetic reel tapes with 1600BPI. BPI stands for bits per inch and it specifies the data density that a magnetic reel can hold. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BPI

title: Graphics Magician Software
description: Early graphics editor. The software is on many disks, each containing a specific component of the editor. It was distributed on 5 and 1/4 inch floppy disks. There are also some disks from the Million Monster Project. Both of these pieces of software date from the early 1980's.

title: VisiCalc Spreadsheeting Disk
description: This 8 inch floppy disk contains an Introduction to VisiCalc Spreadsheeting. It worked for the TRS-80 Model II and Model 16 personal computers. VisiCalc was the first spreadsheet software available for personal computers. The program was initially developed in 1979. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VisiCalc

title: Set of four 256k SIMM
description: A SIMM (single in-line memory module), is a type of memory that contains random access memory (RAM). It was used in computers from the early 1980's to the late 1990's. These particular chips were made by Texas Instruments. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SIMM

title: Minivac 6010
description: The Minivac 6010 is a digital computer kit developed by Scientific Development Corporation. It was released in late 1961. The purpose of the kit was to teach users binary arithmetic, and how computer assembly languages work. The 6010 built on the design of their first product, the 610. The improvements included a new paint job, additional patch cables, and changed the tolerance of some of the switches. As a result the price rose from $85 to $479 for those minor additions in the 6010. The additions were added, so that large companies would purchase them, because they would not purchase the 601. Included is a manual for the 601 that has various learning experiments. References: http://oldcomputermuseum.com/minivac_6010.html http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minivac_601

title: Speed Chassis Bulletin
description: Five bulletins from Specific Products that advertise their speed chassis product, and many accessory components. The speed chassis is used for experimental circuits, temporary apparatus, permanent equipment, bench modes, and rack models.

title: Two Abacuses
description: Counting tool that was first used in China in the 14th century. The top portion of the abacus (the one with fewer beads) is known as heaven, while the bottom portion is known as earth. The beads are counted when they are towards the horizontal beam in the center. The heaven beads are worth five, while the earth beads are worth one. From left to right, the bead values increase by a decimal place (5, 50, 500, 5000, and so on). These basic abacuses can also be used for addition and subtraction. The larger abacus is a traditional Chinese abacus. The smaller one is a Japanese abacus, which was derived from the Chinese version. There methods of operation are virtually identical.

title: TRS-80 Model 100 Portable Computer
description: Sold by Radio Shack, the TRS-80 is considered the first laptop computer. The machine was designed by Kyocera, and released in 1983. Upon release it cost $599. The 32k ROM chip in it was programmed by Bill Gates and Jey Suzuki. This was the last project where Bill Gates wrote the majority of the code. The built-in capabilities of the TRS-80 included Microsoft BASIC programming language, word processing software, telecommunication software, a 300 baud modem. Also included are the machine's quick reference guide, instruction manual, and user's encyclopedia. References: http://oldcomputers.net/trs100.html http://americanhistory.si.edu/collections/comphist/gates.htm#tc34

title: Bohn Contex-10 Desk Calculator
description: A simple manual calculator (no circuitry) that was released in 1957. It could only perform the four basic operations: addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Addition and subtraction are relatively simple operations on the machine, however, multiplication and division are more complex as they require the user to remember the multiplier and quotient in their head. References: http://www.oldcalculatormuseum.com/contex.html

title: Personal Pearl Software and User Manual
description: Personal Pearl is a database program developed in 1982. This specific package came with a Osborne Computer Corporation computer. This information can be deduced from the binder that has written on it, "Approved Software". The package comes with seven floppy disks and a substantial user manual for the system.

title: Personal Pearl Software and User Manual
Personal Pearl Software and User Manual
Wordstar Software
description: Wordstar was a word processor application that was created for the CP/M OS in 1978. It was later ported to the DOS OS. In the early to mid 80's, Wordstar was the dominant word processor on the market. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WordStar

title: Kaypro Perfect Software
description: Office suite created by Perfect Software for Kaypro machines. The software originally shipped with Kaypro machines in the early 80's, but was later replaced by Wordstar software. The software includes Perfect Writer, Perfect Calc, Perfect Speller, Perfect Filer, along with many smaller components. The museum copy does not include the Perfect Writer floppy disk. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kaypro

title: Kaypro Floppy Disks
description: Various boot and utility disks for Kaypro machines during the early 1980's.

title: VisiCalc for Science and Engineering Book
description: VisiCalc was the first spreadsheet program for the personal computer. This book teaches the user how to use it for exercises that relate to Science and Engineering topics. The book was copyrighted in 1983.

title: STARDRIVE: The Wordstar PowerBooster Book
description: WordStar was an early word processor application. Stardrive was a package add on for WordStar. Its capabilities include Graphics, Calculator, Thesaurus, and Dictionary. The book teaches the user how to use this software.

title: WordStar Reference Manual
description: WordStar was a word processor application, created by MicroPro International. This software manual and the software came with a Kaypro machine. The manual teaches the user how to use WordStar. The manual was copyrighted in 1983.

title: GRAFICS 2.0 Instruction Manual
description: GRAFICS 2.0 was a piece of software that was used to produce high resolution graphics. The software shipped with the Osborne 1 personal computer. The manual provides a short introduction on how to use the software efficiently. The manual was copyrighted in 1983.

title: Kaypro User Manuals for the Perfect Software
description: Individual user manual on how to work each individual component of the Perfect Software Suite. Manuals included are for Perfect Calc, Perfect Writer\Speller, and Perfect Filer. The manuals were copyrighted in 1983.

title: Screen-Pac User
description: Screen-Pac was a piece of software for the Osborne 1 personal computer that allowed the user to to configure the video screen. An example of its use would be to increase how much data could be displayed on the screen This guide teaches users how to use the software.

title: Footnote Manual
description: Footnote was a piece of software for the Osborne 1 personal computer that allowed the user to insert footnotes in WordStar text files. The manual teaches users how to use the software.

title: CP/M User Manual
description: CP/M was an early operating system for personal computers that was developed in the mid 1970's. This particular manual came with Kaypro machines.

title: Microsoft BASIC Manual
description: Manual for the BASIC programming languages which was one of the most popular programming languages in the 1970's and 80's. The language was bundled with computers that ran the CP/M operating system.

title: S-BASIC
description: S-BASIC was a version of the BASIC programming language. This manual is from 1979-1980, which indicates that it was later replaced by Microsoft BASIC (its manual dates from 1981) on the CP/M operating system, which ran on Kaypro machines.

title: Kaypro II User Manual
description: Manual that taught users the basics about the Kaypro II personal computer. The Kaypro II was released in 1982.

title: Double Density User Manual
description: Manual for the Osborne 1 personal computer that taught users how to use the double density disk drive option. Double Density enabled users to get twice the amount of storage per disk along with various other smaller upgrades. The manual was copyrighted in 1982.

title: Kaypro Technical Manual
description: Manual for computer repair technicians. It contains diagrams of major components of many Kaypro machines. The level of detail is extreme as the diagrams go all the way down to the gate level (OR, AND, NOT...). The manual was copyrighted in 1984.

title: Apple TV Switch Box and Modulator
description: These two devices were add-ons for the Apple IIc personal computer. The devices allowed the Apple IIc to be displayed on a TV. The modulator piece attached to the back of the computer while the switch box attached to the back of the TV. A video cable then connected the two pieces.

title: Apple TV Switch Box and Modulator
Apple TV Switch Box and Modulator
Hard Sectored Disks
description: Box of 10 floppy disks. These disks are hard sectored which means that they have equally spaced holes on the disk at a common radius. These holes resulted in faster reading and writing time operations. Today, they do not work on most floppy disk drives, because the drive does not have the proper reader to read the holes in the disk.

title: Apple IIc User Manuals
description: A series of manuals for owners of the Apple IIc personal computer. Manuals included are the Owner's Guide, System Utilities, Setup, and Monitor IIc User's Manual.

title: Apple II Plus
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science
description: Personal computer from the famed Apple II series. The Apple II Plus was the second Apple II to be released in the series. The Plus was introduced in June 1979. It was the first in the series to include Applesoft BASIC on the computer without having to purchase an upgrade. Applesoft BASIC became the standard for all subsequent Apple II computers. Otherwise the Plus was identical to the original Apple II. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_II#Apple_II_Plus

title: Apple II Plus
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science

title: Apple II Plus
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science

title: Apple II Plus with Monitor
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science
description: Personal computer from the famed Apple II series. The Apple II Plus was the second Apple II to be released in the series. The Plus was introduced in June 1979. It was the first in the series to include Applesoft BASIC on the computer without having to purchase an upgrade. Applesoft BASIC became the standard for all subsequent Apple II computers. Otherwise the Plus was identical to the original Apple II. This particular Apple II has a monitor attached to it. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_II#Apple_II_Plus

title: Apple IIc
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science
description: An Apple IIc personal computer that was the fourth model to be released in the Apple II series. It was released in April 1984. The purpose of the IIc was portability as the c stands for compact. The idea was that the computer (not including the monitor and power supply) could be transported easily by a single person. The part designed to be portable weighed 7.5 pounds. Also includes power supply and two cables to connect to a monitor. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_IIc

title: Apple IIc
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science

title: Apple IIc
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science

title: Supergrator-2
description: Computing integrator made by Columbia Scientific Industries.

title: XO-1 Laptop
description: Developed by the non-profit One Laptop per Child in 2005, the goal of the product was to make computers available to children around the globe, who would otherwise never be able to have a computer. The product initially was supposed to be $100, but due to manufacturing costs, the product retailed for $199. The computer is designed for low power consumption, and has a rugged design that makes it more durable. It uses flash memory instead of a hard drive and its operating system is a distribution of Linux that is derived from Fedora. The network antenna has an unusual, flip-up style and was designed to support ad-hoc network connections with other similar laptops nearby without the need for an Internet connection. The XO-1 was a major inspiration for netbooks, which were first released in 2007, because of the hardware similarities they share, such as the lack of a optical drive, and the use of flash memory. This computer was purchased in 2007 as part of the "Give 1 Get 1" program. References: Donor Janet Davis http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XO-1_%28laptop%28

title: Heathkit Manual, Illustration Booklets and Schematics for the Model H9 Video Terminal
description: The Heathkit Model H9 Video Terminal is used to display information that comes from either a digital computer or an ASCII keyboard. The information was displayed on a 12 inch cathode ray tube. These various manuals include: 1X Heathkit Manual for the Video Terminal Model H9 8X Pieces of the overall schematics (5 pieces per set, so some are duplicates) 3X Illustration booklets The documentation was copyrighted in 1977.

title: Heathkit Manual, Illustration Booklets and Schematics for the Model H9 Video Terminal
Heathkit Manual, Illustration Booklets and Schematics for the Model H9 Video Terminal
Heathkit Manual, Illustration Booklets and Schematics for the Model H9 Video Terminal
Western Digital Corporation Component Documentation
description: Documentation that contains various schematic diagrams for digital circuits such as Floppy Support Logic and Four Phase Clock Generator. The documentation is from March 1980.

title: Apple Macintosh Performa 6500/224
description: This Apple Macintosh Performa 6500/225 was used by CS faculty member, Henry Walker, from about 1997 through about 2001. The monitor includes built-in speakers — reasonably advanced for the time. The printer comes with a dust cover. Additional peripherals include a standard keyboard and mouse. Additional information supplied by the machine: Operating system: Mac OS 8.1 Build-in memory: 32MB Hard drive: 64MB

title: BetaMax
description: BetaMax was a consumer-level analog videocassette magnetic tape recording format developed by Sony and released on May 10, 1975. BetaMax provided a fine alternative to earlier reel-to-reel systems. Videointerchange.com comments, "Though vastly superior to Vhs in picture quality, it [BetaMax] lost market acceptance mostly through a series of marketing blunders by Sony when JVC introduced the Vhs format a year later. In a side to side comparison of Beta vs Vhs , Beta was the obvious winner - hands down - no contest ! So good was the quality that some broadcasters used it for news gathering as an inexpensive alternative to 3/4" U-matic. Though not true broadcast quality, it was the technically superior consumer format of the time, that should have succeeded much better than it ever did. " On the downside, BetaMax only supported 1 hour long videos, in contrast to the 2-hour long format of VHS,- which was adequate for recording sports games and hollywood movies. Reference: http://www.videointerchange.com/video-history.htm

title: VHS-C Cassette
description: VHS-C was a compact version of the regular VHS format introduced in 1982 and used primarily for consumer-grade compact analog recording camcorders.

title: Apple Studio Display
description: The 16.6" Apple Studio Display supported the Blue & White Power Macintosh G3 computers. Introduced in January 1999, the computer monitor had a distinctive style and color. The monitor attached to a main processor cabinet --- also in the distinctive blue and white color. Typically, the processor unit was placed on the floor, under a desk, so the user could place the display on a desk top with a color-coordinated mouse and keyboard. This model uses the ADC (Apple Display Connector), which carries analog and digital video signals, USB data, and power in the same cable, and is remarkable for being the last CRT-based display that Apple ever shipped. The model was discontinued on May 21, 2001.

title: AppleTalk Cable
description: AppleTalk is Apple's design of a simple, inexpensive and flexible network for connecting computers, peripheral devices, and servers. AppleTalk's flexibility allows it to be used to connect peripherals such as the LaserWriter, or act as a stand-alone local-area network for up to 32 nodes, or form portions of a larger network by using bridges and gateway devices. The AppleTalk Cable is a 6ft long connecting device for the AppleTalk network. Reference: http://www.mactech.com/articles/mactech/Vol.03/03.04/AppleTalkFundamentals/

title: Apple Intelligent Interfaces Communications Interface Card Installation and Operation Manual
description: This softcover manual, produced by Apple Corporation in 1978, gives instructions on the installation and usage of the A2B0003X interface card. Sections of the manual include information on compatibility, basic techniques, advanced techniques, and the parameters of the interface card.

title: Apple (c) Software Bank Contributed Programs Volumes 3-5 Bonus Issue
description: The software manual produced by Apple in 1978 gives information on and instructions on how to run several programs, including information on how to manage the associated products needed to run the programs.

title: Apple Extended Keyboard
description: The Apple Extended Keyboard was released in April 1987. This keyboard was first sold separately alongside the Macintosh II and SE. Later the Apple Extended Keyboard II was prepackaged with Apple Professional Desktops during the early 1990s (starting with the Macintosh IIsi in October 1990). These keyboards are widely considered by enthusiasts to represent the golden era of Macintosh Keyboards and are fondly remembered by many. Retrospective articles credit the success of the product to a combination of sound, feel and durability. Some features of the Apple Extended Keyboard include: caps lock key that physically locks down when activated, which is considered to be better for touch typists, large spacing between keys, especially the top function keys, the width of the keyboard matches the width of the Macintosh II and the height allows it to fit under the "chin" of the Macintosh SE. The Apple Extended Keyboard was discontinued in 1990. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Extended_Keyboard

title: Apple II-6502 Assembly Language Tutor
description: This ring-bound softcover textbook was written by Richard Haskell, and offers instructions on how to operate the software for the Apple II-6502 computer with information on the 3.3 DOS. It includes a 5"x5" floppy disk, and is a second edition, copyright 1983 by Prentice-Hall. The ISBN is 0-13-039230-8.

title: Apple II 80-Column Text Card Manual For //e Only
description: This softcover manual, produced by the Apple Corporation, gives information on how to install and use the 80-column text card. It accompanied the 80-column text card, and is still sealed.

title: Apple II Extended 80-Column Text Card Supplement For //e Only
description: Accompanies the Apple II 80-Column Text Card Supplement. This softcover booklet, produced by the Apple Corporation in 1982, gives information on the background, usage, and installation of auxiliary memory. It also gives programming examples which use auxiliary memory.

title: Apple IIe Owner's Manual
description: The softcover manual with metal ring binding, produced by the Apple Corporation in 1983, accompanies purchase of the Apple IIe (an Apple personal computer). It gives information on assembling the Apple IIe system (unpacking through connecting the monitor), basic system care and use, usage of functions, background on the operating system, usage of the computer, basic information on programming, methods to add more hardware (e.g. printers, cassette recorders, etc), and troubleshooting. The manual also has information in the About section, which indicates which sections are useful for different individuals to read (e.g. the person who sets up the system, first time user, programmer, or business person). References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_//e

title: Apple Macintosh II
description: The Apple Macintosh II was the first personal computer model of the Macintosh II series in the Apple Macintosh line. Introduced in March, 1987, the Macintosh II was the first "modular" Macintosh model, so called because it came in a horizontal desktop case like many PCs of the time. All previous Macintosh computers used an all-in-one design with a built-in black-and-white CRT. The Macintosh II introduced space for an internal hard disk (originally 20 MB or 40 MB) and an optional second floppy disk drive. It also was the first Macintosh computer, simultaneously with the Macintosh SE, to use the Apple Desktop Bus (ADB), introduced with the Apple IIGS, for keyboard and mouse interface Included with the Macintosh II are 9 diskettes which give the user information on installation, printing, disk tools, tidbits and other information about the Macintosh. The Macintosh II was discontinued on January 15, 1990. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Macintosh_II

title: Apple Macintosh II
description: The Apple Macintosh II was the first personal computer model of the Macintosh II series in the Apple Macintosh line. Introduced in March, 1987, the Macintosh II was the first "modular" Macintosh model, so called because it came in a horizontal desktop case like many PCs of the time. All previous Macintosh computers used an all-in-one design with a built-in black-and-white CRT. The Macintosh II introduced space for an internal hard disk (originally 20 MB or 40 MB) and an optional second floppy disk drive. It also was the first Macintosh computer, simultaneously with the Macintosh SE, to use the Apple Desktop Bus (ADB), introduced with the Apple IIGS, for keyboard and mouse interface Included with the Macintosh II are 9 diskettes which give the user information on installation, printing, disk tools, tidbits and other information about the Macintosh. The Macintosh II was discontinued on January 15, 1990. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Macintosh_II

title: Apple Macintosh II
description: The Apple Macintosh II was the first personal computer model of the Macintosh II series in the Apple Macintosh line. Introduced in March, 1987, the Macintosh II was the first "modular" Macintosh model, so called because it came in a horizontal desktop case like many PCs of the time. All previous Macintosh computers used an all-in-one design with a built-in black-and-white CRT. The Macintosh II introduced space for an internal hard disk (originally 20 MB or 40 MB) and an optional second floppy disk drive. It also was the first Macintosh computer, simultaneously with the Macintosh SE, to use the Apple Desktop Bus (ADB), introduced with the Apple IIGS, for keyboard and mouse interface Included with the Macintosh II are 9 diskettes which give the user information on installation, printing, disk tools, tidbits and other information about the Macintosh. The Macintosh II was discontinued on January 15, 1990. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Macintosh_II

title: Apple Macintosh Plus 1Mb
description: The Macintosh Plus computer being the third model in the Macintosh line was introduced on January 16, 1986. It introduced RAM expansion from 1 MB to 4 MB, and the SCSI (Small Computer System Interface) peripheral bus, among smaller improvements. The Macintosh Plus was the last classic Mac to have a phone cord-like port on the front of the unit for the keyboard, as well as the DE-9 connector for the mouse; models released after the Macintosh Plus used ADB (Apple Desktop Bus) ports instead. The Macintosh Plus also had a new 3.5-inch double-sided 800 KB floppy drive, offering double the capacity of previous Macs along with backward compatibility. Unlike earlier Macs, the Mac Plus's keyboard included a numeric keypad and directional arrow keys, and, as with previous Macs, it had a one-button mouse and no fan, making it extremely quiet in operation. The Apple Macintosh Plus was discontinued on October 15, 1990 Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macintosh_Plus

title: Apple (c) Mousepad
description: A gray mousepad with the Apple logo. It has a rubberized bottom to prevent accidental movement of the mousepad, and the top is plastic, so that the mouse can move easily, and the pad can be easily cleaned.

title: Intel 86/380 Computer (Grant O. Gale Observatory): Diskette/Files for Emergency Data Acquisition
description: A diskette with important files for the Intel 86/380 computer in the Gale O. Grant Observatory in case of an emergency. The files on the disk were Varstar, Sr.lst, Photom, Kermit, Reticon, and Image. Kermit was Columbia University's solution for some technical limitation in 1980's. Kermit is a tool for file transfer and mangagement. Varstar was used for photometry, as was sr.lst. Reticon was used for spectroscopy. These programs are mentioned in the “Instructions for SR Star Photometric Observations” and “Notes on Intel 86/380 Computer”. Citations: http://www.columbia.edu/kermit/dec20.html#kermit http://www.columbia.edu/kermit/kermit.html#protocol

title: CISCO BEFSR41 LinkSys Router
description: This router provided switching from one DSL connection to four separate Ethernet cables (no wireless). Details may be found at http://downloads.linksys.com/downloads/userguide/BEFSR41_V43_UG_A-WEB.pdf. John David Stone used this router for his home network approximately 2005-2011.

title: Compaq Safety and Comfort Guide
description: A guide produced by Compaq which gives tips on how to avoid injury and discomfort while working at one's computer station. Includes advice on physical positioning, location of keyboard and monitor, and how to safely use associated products (such as the mouse, keyboard, scanner, or fax machine).

title: Conduit Introductory Mechanics Reviewed and Tested Materials
description: This case contains two 5" floppy disks, which contain the program by Conduit that formerly was used to help teach physical mechanics, for the Physics 354 class at Grinnell College.

title: Dell QuietKey Keyboard
description: A traditional QWERTY keyboard, produced by Dell. This model has retractable tabs to allow the keyboard to rest at different angles for easier typing. Most often accompanies Windows 95/98 or later models.

title: Digital Keyboard Model PC7XL-AA
description: A standard QWERTY keyboard, this model is designed on an incline for easy typing, and has plastic hinges that allow the user to give the keyboard more of an incline. This model also has the keys F1-F12, the number pad, and lights which show whether or not Num Lock, Caps Lock, or Scroll Lock are on.

title: Digital Computer Monitor Model VT100-AA
description: This monitor, manufactured in March 1992, takes in several inputs: video cords in and out, keyboard, mouse, and monitor. The top of the monitor can be locked in place or unlatched by the flip of a switch, located on the back of the monitor. The computer has a 6"x10" screen.

title: Digital Mouse Model PC7XS
description: A mouse, produced by the Digital Corporation. It has two mouse buttons (left and right), and is a 'ball mouse' (it has a rubber ball inside which registers movement).

title: Digital PCXCV-AC Monitor
description: Digital PCXCV-AC Monitor is a 15-inch, 13.8" viewable, auto-scanning color monitor with high resolution, compatible with VGA (Video Graphics Array), SVGA (Super Video Graphics Array), and 1024x768 modes. The PCXCV-AC has 1024 horizontal x 768 vertical non-interlaced resolution, power management system that exceeds EPA ENERGY STARTM requirements for saving energy, VESA Data Display Channel - DDC1/2b, anti-glare screen with anti-static coating, tilt-swivel base and it also complies with Swedish MPR2 standards for low magnetic emissions. Reference: www.compaq.com/legacysupport/digital/pdf/xcvtx-im.pdf

title: Digital Software Rainbow 100 MBASIC-86 Reference Manual
description: The Rainbow 100 Reference Manual was produced by Microsoft Corporation, accompanying Digital Corporation hardware. This manual gives information on usage of the MBASIC-86 programming language. The softcover ring-bound manual gives general information about MBASIC-86, commands and statements, functions, and information on troubleshooting and the meaning of error codes. The manual was copyrighted in 1983. This manual accompanies the Digital Software Rainbow Owner's Manual.

title: Digital Software Rainbow Owner's Manual
description: This softcover manual, produced in 1983 by the Digital Equipment Corporation, accompanies purchase of the Rainbow computer. The manual gives information on the computer, the keyboard, computer set-up, connecting a printer, communicating with another computer, testing the computer, and troubleshooting.

title: Digital Set Up Keyboard Model AB153
description: This keyboard is set to both normal usage, as well as specific programming usage. This keyboard can be set to 80 or 132 characters per line, certain key combinations perform functions, extra keys perform functions, and instead of a F1-F12 bar, there is a "function" bar set above the number line. This keyboard has the standard QWERTY setup, as well as a number pad. The keys of this keyboard are easily removable and replaceable.

title: Digital VT300-Family Supporting Products Guide
description: This booklet gives information on the products of the Digital Corporation VT300 line, especially which products are used in conjunction. The softcover guide is sealed in a plastic case to protect against damage.

title: Alps Mechanical Mouse 500 Series
description: The 500 Series model of the Alps mouse rolls over a rubber ball to sense movement. It has both left- and right-click functions and was made in the USA.

title: Fujitsu Keyboard Model FKB4870
description: This keyboard has the standard QWERTY setup, as well as a number pad.

title: Dysan Corporation Diskette
description: A container made by the Dysan Corporation to hold diskettes (the precursor to floppy disks). Contains seven diskettes. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetBIOS

title: Epson Stylus 740 Color Inkjet Printer
description: Inkjet printers print digital images by propelling ink droplets of varying sizes onto a page. These are currently the most common form of printer. Inkjet technology was first developed in the 1950s, although they weren't mass-produced until the 1970s. Epson is one of the top four main injet printer producers. This printer works with Windows 95/98, Apple MacOS 7.0, or Microsoft Windows 4.0 or later. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inkjet_printer http://www.epson.com/cgi-bin/Store/support/supDetail.jsp?

title: Digital Computer Monitor Model VR241-A
description: This monitor, produced in 1984 by the Hitachi Corporation, is an example of an older desktop-sized computer monitor. Brightness and color contrast adjustment wheels are on the side of the monitor, with the on/off switch located on the back, near the RGB input/output wire locations.

title: NeXTSTEP Software Release Version 0.9
description: This operating system, developed by NeXT Computer Company to run on a range of workstation computers, is a multitasking operating system that is object oriented. It combines the Unix OS, with the Objective-C programming language, as well as tools to modify the included object-oriented application layers. The first version, 1.0, was released in 1989, after several previews as early as 1986. Thus, the 0.9 release version was a "beta program", which was supplied to beta testers to look for errors in the programming. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NeXTSTEP

title: NeXTSTEP Software Release Version 0.8a
description: This operating system, developed by NeXT Computer Company to run on a range of workstation computers, is a multitasking operating system that is object oriented. It combines the Unix OS, with the Objective-C programming language, as well as tools to modify the included object-oriented application layers. The first version, 1.0, was released in 1989, after several previews as early as 1986. Thus, the 0.8a release version was a "beta program", which was supplied to beta testers to look for errors in the programming. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NeXTSTEP

title: NeXTSTEP 256 Megabyte Optical Disk
description: This disk was produced by NeXTSTEP Computing using magneto-optics to create a removable read/write/erasable standard mass storage device. These were mostly produced in the late 80s, although a few were in use in the 90s, and were considered an innovation in their time. They were inspired by the concept of a "digital library", in which online works were easily cataloged, searched for, and indexed. References: http://skytel.co.cr/bsd/research/1988/1012.htm

title: Macintosh System 7 Release 7.1 (Beta)
description: This beta-version operating system (version 7.1) comes on a CD, with an instructions booklet. This particular OS (operating system), designed by the Apple Developer Group, is only compatible with Macintosh computers.

title: Stepstone ICpak v1.0 & v1.1 GRAPHpak Library
description: The program versions contained on these tapes were manufactured in March 1990 as the Beta versions of the GRAPHpak Library. They were specifically designed for the Sun 3 SunOS 4.0 with SunWindows (SunOS being the operating system version 4.0, with the graphical user interface SunWindows). Each tape itself, produced by the 3M Company, is a DC 300A data cartridge, with 300 feet of reel, playing at 1600 bpi (bits per inch, bits being the well-known "0s and 1s", building blocks of programming). References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_cartridge

title: Stepstone ICpak v1.0 & v1.1 GRAPHpak Library
Stepstone ICpak v1.0 & v1.1 GRAPHpak Library
Kas-Ette/10 Library Case
description: A beige, hard plastic case that snaps closed, this case is designed to hold (with a flexible holding system) fourteen eight-inch floppy disks. Floppy disks used to be the most popular and common way to store data, until the advent of CDs, DVDs, flash drives, and memory cards. The original floppy disks were designed by IBM, and became commercially available in 1971. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floppy_disk#History

title: Microtek SlimScan C2
description: A flatbed scanner capable of connecting to a computer using a USB connection. This type of scanner was almost universally used for home/small business usage. This model is capable of use with either a PC or a Mac. It is very compact; only 1"x11"x18". References: http://support.microtek.com/product_dtl_2.phtml?prod_id=91

title: MX-100 Epson Dot Matrix Printer Operation Manual
description: Dot matrix printers operate by moving a printer head up and down the page (or side to side), and having an ink-soaked cloth ribbon (studded with tiny pins) strike the paper. The pins (guided by a small guide plate) strike the paper in a format to print letters and characters of varying sizes and fonts. The manual for the MX-100 Epson Dot Matrix printer explains background, installation, and operation of the printer. The manual was produced in 1981 by Epson Company. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_matrix_printer

title: Synertek Incorporated Programming Manual
description: This manual, produced by Synertek Incorporated, is known as the SY6500/MCS6500 programming manual. The contents of this manual, as explained in the introduction, are subject to a cross-licensing agreement between Synertek and MOS Technology; thus, the manual should cover all 65XX microprocessors for both companies. The manual covers the concepts of the data bus, accumulator, arithmetic unit, flags and status registers, tests, branches, jump instructions, stack processing, index registration, resetting and interrupting, shift, memory modification, and peripheral programming. This manual was printed in August 1976.

title: Rainbow Product Guide December 1985
description: This booklet is the product guide for the items produced by the Rainbow Company, including both the hardware and software options. The booklet also gives instructions on how to order the items, what operating systems they are compatible with, and the available customizable options. Interested observers should note the prices for computers during this time (around $4,000 to $6,000 each during 1985), and compare prices to the computers of today, which have vastly more memory and capabilities.

title: Mountain Computer Incorporated A/D + D/A Operating Manual
description: This manual gives instructions on how to install and use the A/D + D/A board. The A/D + D/A board converts data from analog to digital, as well as from digital to analog; the board is used in conjunction with an Apple II or Apple II Plus computer. The importance of the A/D and D/A board is that it allows interaction with multiple sources of data, both for input and output of data. This manual, copyrighted in 1981, gives information on how to manipulate the use of the board to its highest potential.

title: Sytek EPROMS Chips with Version 2.2.5 Software Upgrade Installation Instructions
description: As can be deduced by the title, these computer chips are to be installed in the Sytek Localnet Communications box, in order to implement the new version of the software. The instruction manual accompanying the chips explains the proper disassembly/installation/reassembly procedures. The chips and manual were made in 1984 by Sytek, Incorporated.

title: ArithmaTHINK Mathematical Early Education Kit
description: This kit contains nine manuals regarding use of the ArithmaTHINK kit, as well as an "arithmetic board", many puzzle pieces for the board, a notepad to keep track of results, and several wheels with mathematical equations. All of these are used in conjunction to help a parent home-school their child in mathematics. This manuals are all copyrighted in 1968, although the kit was produced in 1969.

title: USB cord
description: The design of USB is standardized by USB Implementers Forum (USB-IF). The USB (Universal Serial Bus) cord is used to establish communication between devices and the computer.

title: Instruction Manual: Microsoft Windows 95 Operating System
description: This Instruction Manual comes with the Microsoft Windows 95 Operating System and also a Certificate of Authenticity. The manual explains the steps involved in the installation of Windows 95 on the COMPAQ PC.

title: COMPAQ: Contacting Customer Support
description: This Guide provides the user with worldwide telephone numbers for Compaq assistance. This guide was written in 10 different languages which are English, Portuguese, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Dutch, French, German, Italian, Japanese and Spanish.

title: COMPAQ Troubleshooting Guide
description: This guide provides the user with information on how to solve various problems on the Compaq Deskpro Family of Personal Computers, such as Disk problems, Display problems, Audio problems, Printer problems, Hardware Installation problems, Memory problems and Software problems.

title: S-PLUS 5 Manual
description: This manual provides information on getting started with S-PLUS 5.1, a commercial implementation of the S-Programming Language. S-PLUS features object-oriented programming capabilities and advanced analytical algorithms. This manual helps the user understand the basic features of S-PLUS 5.1.

title: BLASTER CD-ROM Installation Disk
description: The CD-ROM Installation Disk contains a README file that helps the user install the CREATIVE BLASTER CD on the computer.

title: EtherDisk XL
description: This Disk contains drivers for NetWare 3.x and 4.x, DOS, Windows, Windows NT, Windows 95, and other NDIS-based (Network Driver Interface Specification) Network Operating Systems. It also includes utilities such as AutoLink, for Auto Installation in NetWare Clients, and DynamicAccess Features.

title: HP Colorado 5GB and 8GB Internal tape Drives
description: The tape Drive comes with an Install Assistant Software in form of a diskette and an Installation Guide. The Guide provides the user with information on various tape backup solutions,

title: COMPAQ Restore CD
description: This recovery CD helps the user restore previous computer software to its original state. It enables the user to restore the system files from a separate partition on the hard drive.

title: COMPAQ Safety & Comfort Guide
description: This comfort guide provides the user with information on how to place input devices such as the keyboard and mouse. It also explains how the hands and arms should be placed around different devices, as well as the viewing angle of the display and health habits. The Guide also gives information on installation of COMPAQ products.

title: EtherLink XL PCI User Guide
description: This Guide describes how to install, configure, and troubleshoot the 3Com EtherLink XL Parallel Tasking PCIBus Master Network Interface Cards (NICS). It also provides the user with maintenance and safety informations.

title: MINITAB Mini-Manual
description: Minitab is a statistics package. It was developed at the Pennsylvania State University by researchers Barbara F. Ryan, Thomas A. Ryan, Jr., and Brian L. Joiner in 1972. Minitab began as a light version of OMNITAB, a statistical analysis program by NIST (National Institute of Standards and technology). Today, Minitab is often used in conjunction with the implementation of Six Sigma, CMMI and other statistics-based process improvement methods. This Mini-Manual serves as a guide to users. It explains the fundamentals of using minitab such as how to enter commands, how to manage data and files, how to analyze data and how to produce graphs. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology

The Expansion Unit 5161 provided one or two 10MB hard disks (this unit has two), increasing the storage capacity of the original IBM PC by about 62.5 times.

Their approach was to supply components in kit form, allowing individuals to assemble the pieces into functioning units. Equipment included audio equipment (e.g., amplifiers) and electrical units. The FLEX Operating System was designed for the 6809 CPU (Central Processing Unit) chip. System requirements inlcuded 12K random access memory (RAM) with specific memory accresses additional main memory (48K) making 56K memory total When running, the operating system occupied the top 12K of memory (addresses 48K to 56K) 2 disk drives, connected to a controller and addressed as drive #0 and drive #0

title: Seagate Barracuda Hard Drive 7200.10
description: Built by Seagate in about 2001, this hard drive stores 500GB of data. The drive gets its model number (7200) from the speed at which the disk rotates: 7200 RPM. The device had several features (e.g., cache and storage configuration) that represented high-performance storage at the time it was manufactured.

It has left-, right-, and middle-click buttons and displays the CAChe chemistry set logo at the trackball's base. The full CAChe system package includes a Macintosh computer with upgraded coprocessor card, a 3D stereoscopic display, and the 3D trackball and can be found on display at the Tektronix Museum in Portland, Oregon. This compact home system opens to provide a screen, a general-purpose editing keyboard, and adjustable crossfade, level and balance control functions. Sony introduced DAT cassettes as a competitor format to general audio cassettes. However, concerns from the music industry about the the potential use of DAT cassettes to reproduce copy-right music with no loss of quality prevented DAT cassettes from catching on. title: Pascal Language Handbook
description: The Pascal Language Handbook by Michael L. Hall and Robert D. Reimiller. The original Pascal programming language was developed in the early 1970s by Niklaus Wirth and implemented on a CSC 6000 computer in Zurich, Switzerland. The version of Pascal discussed here was implemented by OMEGASOFT, Sunnyvale, California, and apparently targeted an 8-bit 6809 micro-computer. Some "extensions" of the original Pascal include a STRING data type. Since the target machine is small, the PACK and UNPACK statements of original Pascal are not supported; some I/O details also may vary from commonly used versions of Pascal. This second printing of the manual is dated January, 1982.

title: PowerBook 159
description: The powerbook was introduced on October 19, 1992. It featured a 25 Mhz 68030 processor, 4 MB of RAM, and came with the option of 40, 80 or 120 MB of hard drive space. Also it came with a 1.44 MB floppy drive and a 640 x 400 pixel LCD. The screen was back lit by a cold cathode fluorescent lamp. The display supports 4-bit gray-scale but can support 8-bit color display on all Apple monitors up to 16 inches. This was the first power book to do so. It retailed at $2430. A few changes from the the older models were that the screen supports 1, 2, and 4 bit modes. Also the frame buffer was moved from $FE00 0000 to $6000 0000 and also occupies 128KB instead of 32KB. Another change was the display used a fixed device, instead of a color look up table(CLUT). This would effect software attempting to change the color table. This Powerbook also has a soft shut down feature. This means that that pressing the button the back would the same as manually shut the laptop off from the start menu. Joyce Stern used this specific Powerbook 160. She is presently the “dean of student academic support and advising at Grinnell College”. The Powerbook was bought in late 1992 when she started at Lawrence University and the first computer she had ever owned. It died around 2000. Cite: http://www.everymac.com/systems/apple/powerbook/specs/mac_powerbook160.html Cite: Developer Note: Macintosh PowerBook 160 and Macintosh PowerBook 180

description: This Intel 86/380 computer was given to the observatory by Bob Noyce in 1983, when the observatory was under construction and Intel was still making complete computer systems. It served as the observatory’s primary data acquisition and analysis machine until 1996. The Intel computer handled almost all computing tasks except control of the telescope, including the digitizing of signals from instruments, control of instrumentation, acquisition of images, and a wide variety of data analysis and display tasks. The hardware consists of two units; one contains the processor, memory, display controller, I/O, and frame grabber circuit boards, while the other contains the hard disk and an 8” floppy disk drive. The system has 768 kB of memory and 35 MB of disk space. The Intel 8086 processor runs at 5 MHz. In spite of these limitations it served observatory users well. User interaction with the computer is through a terminal, a printer, a color video display, and a light pen that provides mouse-like functions in conjunction with the video display. The 86/380 is a single-user system that runs Intel’s iRMX operating system. Almost all of its application software was written at Grinnell, mostly in Pascal, which was the language that Grinnell students learned in computing courses at that time. At the time of its removal from service the 86/380 was relatively healthy and a brief document was prepared to assist anyone who might wish to bring it back to life. --R. Cadmus 7/27/13.

The diskettes include, the iRMX 860 Utilities Package, Word Processing System Dskette(copy 1), Word Processing: The Basics 'DOCBAC' , Word Processing: The Basics Document Copy Practice, Word Processing System Diskette, Word Processing – The Basics Diversify Gives Errors, iRMX 860, ASM 86 and Numerics Library, iRMX 863 Pl?m 86/88/186, iRMX 860 Utilities Package, iRMX 860 ASM 86, iRMX 863 PL/M 86/88, and Word Processing: Advanced Training Diskette 'ADVBAC'.

title: Intel SSBC 534 Communications Expansion Board (Grant O. Gale Observatory)
description: The Intel SSBC 534 Communications Expansion Board is described as a "Multibus | Single Board Computer" and as a "Communication Controller". The board supports four fully programmable synchronous and asynchronous serial communications channels. Each serial I/O channel provides 16 maskable interrupt request lines and two independent programmable 16-bit interval timers.

title: Kensington Mouse-in-a-Box
description: The Kensington Mouse-in-a-Box has ADB connections for new and old Macs. It also uses an optical technology for more precise tracking. It uses an easy plug-and-play with no software required and It has a symmetrical design for both right- and left-hand comfort. Reference: http://us.kensington.com/html/6389.html

title: Hi8 (High Band 8mm Video Tape)
description: Hi8 (High Band Video 8) was an improvement to the Video-8, first version of 8mm video tapes. They were used primarily in camcorders as well as some professional television production. Sony released the Hi8 format in 1989 in response to Super VHS by JVC.

title: MacRecorder
description: The MacRecorder audio digitizer was invented to give SoundEdit, which was the first popular GUI-based audio editor for digitized audio in 1985, in-built sound input. MacRecorder was created by Michael Lamoureux, a mathematics student at the University of California, Berkeley. The MacRecorder hardware and software was publicly released through the Berkeley Macintosh Users Group as a kit in late 1985. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SoundEdit

title: Macally iKey Slim USB Keyboard
description: This 2012 keyboard came as part of a low-end Linux desktop workstation. Nevertheless, the iKey Slim keyboard emulates a standard Macintosh keyboard, and used a USB connector.

title: Microcom AX/2400C Modem
description: This modem communicated over dial-up telephone lines with a speed of 2400 baud. The modem used used the Hayes command set, a common protocol developed in 1981 and used widely during the 1990s. John David Stone used this modem at home approximately 1990-2004. Originally, this was used with a DEC Rainbow computer to connect with a modem on one of the MathLAN servers.

title: Motorola Cell Phone TELE T-A-C 249
description: The Motorola TELE T-A-C 250 represents a first generation cellular telephone. The hand held device communicated over an analog cellular network (now called a 1G network). Over time, analog communication has been replaced by digital cellular networks, making analog communication obsolete. In rural areas, cellular telephones were particularly popular with people who regularly traveled over long distances between cities or in areas of low population. Mobile telephones provided a measure of security and safety in the case of a vehicle breakdown in an isolated area. This telephone was purchased by Joyce Stern in 1998, when she was commuting to work.

title: Logitech M-CW46
description: The Logitech M-CW47 Mouse was manufactured by Logitech in 1998. It has a 331054-1011 C4.1 Microchip and 5 SMD (Surface Mount Device) transistors Refrences: http://www.tcocd.de/Pictures/Peripheral/Logitech/mcw47.shtml http://www.tcocd.de/Pictures/Peripheral/Logitech/mcw47-4_large.jpg

title: Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
description: Manufactured in the early 2000s, the Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer advertised, " incredible speed and performance in the home or office, whether you're printing photos, presentations or text. You get phenomenal plain paper print outs. And, EPSON DuraBrite Inks mean the results are both water- and light-resistant", according to its Information Sheet from Epson [1] "Key Features" from the Information sheet include: Optimized for plain paper printing Delivers exceptional results for both photos and everyday printing Water- and light-resistant DuraBriteTM Inks Light resistance up to 80 years on specialty papers and 70 years on plain paper* Cost-saving individual ink cartridges 5760 x 1440 optimized dpi using Resolution Performance ManagementTM (RPMTM) Optimizes the placement of six ink droplet sizes at resolutions up to 5760 x 1440 dpi on various papers for the best detail Up to 22 ppm black text and 11 ppm color printing** Network and wireless options 802.11b Wireless Print Server - Bluetooth Print Adapter from Epson EpsonNet 10/100Base-TX Type B Ethernet Print Server" [1] The printer came with software drivers which worked with Windows® USB (98, 2000, Me and XP),Windows Parallel (95, 98, 2000, NT 4.0, Me and XP), and Macintosh® USB (8.5.1 - 9.x, OS X 10.1 or later). The printer accommodated several standard, pre-set paper sizes, and also allowed user-defined paper sizes from 3.5" x 3.5" up to 8.5" x 44". Reference: Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer Information Sheet, http://www.epson.com/cmc_upload/0/00/013/482/SC82_InfoSheet.pdf. Retrieved 27 June 2013.

title: User's Guide Compaq S710 Color Monitor
description: Several small booklets that accompany the Compaq S710 Color Monitor which provide information about how to connect the monitor to the system, adjusting the settings, advise for proper use, and warnings about dangers associated with improper usage (such as electrical shock). Written in multiple languages.

title: Princeton Graphic Systems MAX-12 Single Phase Monitor
description: This monitor, produced by Princeton Graphic Systems (PGC), has a screen size of 6"x10". It has two knobs which are pulled out and turned to adjust either brightness or contrast. It was manufactured in August 1984, and is capable of working on AC (alternating current) 60Hz (Hertz), 120V (volts), 40W (watts).

title: HP 9000 715 Server 1.0GB Single Ended SCSI hard drive
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science
description: In 1994, Grinnell's Department of Mathematics and Computer Science upgraded its local area network, MathLAN, from Sun 3/50 computers to HP 712 computers. In the new configuration, users interacted directly with 55 Hewlett-Packard 712/60 workstations. These were connected to MathLAN and an HP 715/75 file server. For its day, the server had significant capacity with 8 gigabytes of storage capacity. The HP 715/75 file server utilized several disk drives, including this hard drive. These drives implemented the Small Computer System Interface, or SCSI, for transferring data between disk and computers.

title: Apple Centronics Printer Card
description: This integrated circuit fits on a board that plugged into an Apple II computer. The board provided the logic and circuitry to connect the computer to a separate printer. The packaging on this equipment is dated 1979.

title: Microtype Tutor
description: Microtype Tutor is a package of typing exercises and software to help students improve their typing skills. A description in the 1989 catalog of J. Weston Walch (p. 11) states, "Advanced students can polish their skills with this package of disk-and-worksheet exercises. The disk contains 4 student programs: Home-Base Letters (two-letter home row exercises); Words and Phrases (typing reach drills); Finger Placement; and Speed." Students would type material from worksheets at a computer keyboard, and a program would monitor speed and accuracy. This package includes a full Walch catalog and numerous worksheets for student use. Microtype Tytor was written by John B. Cook.

title: Viking Memory
description: Many computers come with a basic level of memory, but can be expanded by plugging additional memory cards into slots within the computer. The chips here, together with the accompanying instruction booklet, provide expansion memory for AcerNote Light computers.

title: HP 9000 715 Server 1.0GB Single Ended SCSI hard drive
donor: Department of Mathematics and Computer Science
description: In 1994, Grinnell's Department of Mathematics and Computer Science upgraded its local area network, MathLAN, from Sun 3/50 computers to HP 712 computers. In the new configuration, users interacted directly with 55 Hewlett-Packard 712/60 workstations. These were connected to MathLAN and an HP 715/75 file server. For its day, the server had significant capacity with 8 gigabytes of storage capacity. The HP 715/75 file server utilized several disk drives, including this hard drive. These drives implemented the Small Computer System Interface, or SCSI, for transferring data between disk and computers.

title: SanDisk Sansa m230
description: The SanDisk Sansa m230 is an MP3 player with 512 Megabyte memory. Developed in 2004, the digital player supports the MP3 audio standards and runs on a AAA battery. Reviews suggest very good tone quality, light weight (1.3 ounces), and moderate size (3.1 by 1.7 inches).

title: Computer Museum Time Capsule
description: The Computer Museum compiled a Time Capsule — a collection of artifacts from the Museum, together with a guide describing each piece. The following items are paired with the pictures that follow. The Time Capsule Guide is a descriptive booklet to the various artifacts included in the time capsule. Vacuum Tubes acted as electrical switches in the early computers. The post card here shows part of the 5,000 vacuum tubes found in the 1951 Whirlwind computer. Paper Tape: Used between the 1940s and 1970s, data were encoded on paper tape by punching holes at specific locations; each letter or digit gave rise to a specific pattern of punches. Sequences of instructions were represented by sequences of punch codes. Of course, if any data needed to be added or deleted, an entire, new tape had to be repunched to effect the change. Magnetic Tape: This magnetic tape, from a 1950s computer, stores data as magnetic charges. To maintain a magnetic charge, the tape is coated with iron filings. For reference a bag of iron filings is also shown. Transistors, made of silicon, were developed in the 1950s and 1960s to replace vacuum tubes. Since transistors do not heat a filament (found in vacuum tubes), transistors utilize much less electricity and create much less heat than vacuum tubes. Transistors have three wire connects — a control wire in the middle and two other wires to complete a circuit. Minicomputer Module: In 1960s computers, placed transistors on boards, connected by resistors (round, stripped) and capacitors (thin, square). Read Only Memory (ROM) stores program information, replacing punched cards, tape, or other media for relatively fast access.

title: DEC Personal Computer 433dxLP
description: In the mid 1990s, Digital Equipment Corporation produced several lines of personal computers. The DECpc LP series were considered high performance machines and could be used either in stand-along mode or within a networked environment. All DECpc LP computers were based on Intel's i80486 chip. This machine has model number PC743.
