Basic specifications for all DECpc LP machines include: Three ISA expansion slots Dual IDE hard disk drive interface 32-bit local bus SVGA controller with GUI accelerator and 512KB VRAM standard

title: Continuous-feed printer paper
description: Most printers in the 1970s and 1980s utilized paper with holes punched along the sides. The paper fed into a sprocket assembly, and moving sprockets engaged the holes to move the paper in a controlled way. The paper itself was a long, continuous piece. Perforations on the sides allowed the punched strips on the sides to be removed easily. Perforations between pages allowed output to be separated easily into individual pages. Pages for this paper measures 9.5" by 11", including the 0.5" strips with punched holes on each side. Removing the punched strips yields a standard 8.5" by 11" page. This paper was manufactured by Willamette Industries, Inc. for the LA50 printer.

title: iRMX KERMIT
description: KERMIT is a widely-used file-transfer program that allows users to move files from one computer system to another. These diskettes contain version 2.42 of KERMIT, for use with the iRMX operating system.

title: 26-wire Ribbon Cable
description: Ribbon cable provides parallel wires to connect computer equipment; often 26-wire ribbon cable connects computers to printers. This 26-wire ribbon cable has a 32-pin SCSI connector (blue) at one end and a 28-pin printer connector at the other. At each end, unneeded pins are unused in connecting to the ribbon cable. This ribbon cable was manufactured by Don Connex, has part number E152690, and extends about 6 feet.

title: Microscience HH 612 Hard Drive
description: This Microscience HH 612 hard drive has a capacity of 10 Megabytes. Data are stored on both sides of the disk, and two read/write heads access the data on each side. Data are store in 17 sectors, 306 cylinders. This drive was donated in 1994.

title: Microscience HH 612 Hard Drive
description: This Microscience HH 612 hard drive has a capacity of 10 Megabytes. Data are stored on both sides of the disk, and two read/write heads access the data on each side. Data are store in 17 sectors, 306 cylinders. This drive was donated in 1994.

title: Paper tape
description: For many early computers, input was accomplished through holes punched onto paper tape. The tape itself was either stacked up layer after layer or rolled up on a spool. This roll of 1/2" paper tape would extend hundreds of yards if completely unwound.

title: Apple II Manuals and Software
description: The Department of Psychology used Apple II computers in their labs for a variety of experiments. As with many computers, the Apple II's were accompanied by a variety of manuals and software packages. These include the following. The DOS Manual, describing the Disk Operating System from Apple II The Apple II Reference Manual The Basic Programming Reference Manual and quick reference cards, describing Applesoft II BASIC for the Apple II. A variety of 5 1/2" diskettes with software and data.

title: Apple II Diskettes and Instruction Manual for Fourier Analysis I and II
description: The instruction booklet describes a two-diskette package "for the study and application of Fourier analysis" (introduction, p. v). The software and booklet, written by Scott Stevens, is designed to run on an Apple II-plus with 48K memory. 'Game paddles must be in use when using either of these diskettes. Failure to install the paddles will result in a "crash." ' The booklet provides explanatory information, a range of graphs, and detailed instructions on using the accompanying mathematical software package.

title: Core Memory
description: Early computer memory utilized a grid of wires; a row in the grid could represent a collection of bits (e.g., a byte or word), and different rows represented different addresses of memory. Two wires connected to each bit, and pulses of electricity along a row and a column allowed reading or writing to a specific bit.

title: SIGGRAPH 1990 Lanyard and Clasp
description: SIGGRAPH, a Special Interest Group of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM), holds conferences that focus on developments within the field of computer graphics. A lanyard and clasp were designed for the 1990 SIGGRAPH conference, held in Dallas, Texas.

title: iRMX 863 Software
description: The iRMX operating system was designed for real-time computing using Intel 8088 and Intel 8086 processors. These diskettes provide software within this environment. The iRMX 863 PL/M-86 compiler provided programming support for the PL/M programming language. This diskette is dated 1981, 1982. The iRMX PSCOPE 86 software provided a high-level program debugger within this environment. This diskette is a Demo disk.

title: Apple iMAC I/O Software
description: Drivers (special-purpose software) were needed when connecting peripheral equipment to the iMac computers of 1998-1999, drivers. Cables would provide a physical connection between the computer and the external hardware, but the drivers allowed the computer to control printing or other information transfer. The software on these CDs provides this software support for two types of equipment. The HP Printer Cable Kit for iMac Starter CD for Hewlett Packard printers The SuperDisk USB Drive for Macintosh Installation CD and User's Guide for the Imation SuperDisk.

title: epi-View by Picker
description: From the accompanying directions: "Picker's epi-View surgical reference view is a method of displaying a 4-D angiography image on file with striking 3-D anatomic perspective without special workstation requirements." Negatives are produced at about the size 14" (wide) by 18" (high), and these are inserted in to a plexiglas frame. When light is placed behind the unit, the negatives yield a 3-dimensional effect.

title: S Word Processing Package
description: The S Word Processing package could be used in an MS-DOS environment. This disk contains version 2.2 from August 1987.

title: Binary number cards
description: These cards use holes and slots to represent the numbers 1 to 15. In the binary representation, a 0 is represented by a hole in the card; a 1 by a slot from the hole-position to the edge of the card. Inserting pins into the holes, corresponding to a binary representation, one can retrieve the card with a desired number. These cards were used for class demonstration by Professor Emily Moore, based on a reference by Martin Gardner.

title: TRS-80 User's Encyclopedia
description: The back cover of this book provides this description: "the complete ready reference that is a must for every TRS-80 Color Computer owner. This comprehensive book will answer your questions, give you "inside" information, and greatly increase your use and enjoyment of your computer. It covers every aspect of using the Color Computer. Explains programming languages, including BASIC Guides you through DOS, ASCII, FLEX, etc. Simplifies operating procedures Describes hundreds of software packages and hardware accessories, with detailed vendor information Lists publications, users' groups, and other information sources Clarifies general microcomputer terminology and concepts And much more."

title: IBM System/360 Reference Card
description: The IBM System/360 family provided a standard, upward-compatible series of computer systems that were particularly popular within the business world. Programming often involved the use of assembly language, and the System/360 Reference Data Card or "Green Card" provided a handy reference to the various assembly instructions and technical details needed for programming.

title: SanDisk Sansa e140
description: The SanDisk Sansa e140 is an MP3 player with 1 Gigabyte memory. A product description on Amazon.com states, "Combining great features and pocket-friendly compactness, SanDisk Sansa digital audio players make it possible to get the most out of your music. Not only is the design great, and the sound quality exceptional, but SanDisk players are also affordable, making it easy to get your groove on wherever you roam. The Sansa e140 Digital Audio Player features a built-in digital FM tuner you can preset 20 of your favorite radio stations. Even better, in addition to the 1 GB of built-in storage space, you can add up to 2 GB of extra storage space with the included SD slot so you'll have plenty of room for your favorite MP3, Audible, and WMA music."

title: Punch cards with a FORTRAN Coding Form
description: In the 1960s and 1970s, programs were punched on cards that they could be read into a computer. Each card contained one line of a program, with one character represented in each of 80 columns. Different punches represented different characters. When programming in the FORTRAN programming language, specific columns of a card were used for different purposes. For example, columns 1 through 5 were used to assign a number to a statement in case it needed to be referenced elsewhere; a character in column 6 indicated the material on this card continued work started on the previous card; regular instructions or commands were placed in columns 7 through 72; and (optionally) sequencing information was placed in columns 73-80. In order to keep track of what should be typed where, it was not uncommon for programmers to first write their programs on special coding forms. Each form allowed the writing of several lines (e.g., 24 lines in this case), and lines were divided into columns that corresponded to locations on a punch card. This coding form was used by John Stone during a course he took in 1972. The blank punch card comes from the University of Iowa Computation Center, and the punched card from the Computer Museum, then located in Boston, MA.

title: Netgear Web Safe Router RP614 v2
description: The Netgear Web Safe Router RP614 v2 provides switching capability among 4 local port cables and a DSL cable. The instruction manual lists these "Key Features". "The RP614 v2 router provides you with multiple Web content filtering options, plus browsing activity reporting and instant alerts -- both via e-mail. Parents and network administrators can establish restricted access policies based on the time-of-day, Website addresses and address keywords, and share high-speed cable/DSL Internet access for up to 253 personal computers. Network Address Translatio (NAT) protects you from hackers. With minimum setup, you can install and use the router within minutes. The RP614 v2 router proves the following features: Easy, web-based setup for installation and management Stateful Packet Inspection, Content Filtering, and Site Blocking Security Built-in 4-port 10/100 Mbps Switch Ethernet connection to a wide area network (WAN) device, such as a cable modem or DSL modem Extensive Protocol Support Login capability Front panel LEDs for easy monitoring of status and activity Flash memory for firmware upgrade"

title: IBM Flowcharting Template
description: Flowcharting was a popular way to describe algorithms and processes, particularly in the 1970s. Each type of operation was represented by a specific shape, as described by several national standards. For example, "processing, annotation" was placed in a rectangle, a magnetic tape was represented by a circle, a perforated tape by a rectangular region with wavy sides, and punched cards by a rectangle with a corner removed. The IBM Flowcharting Template, Form X20-8020, collected many of these standard shapes in an easily used format for use in specifying algorithms and programs. This item dates from approximately 1972. The paper cover contains summary information regarding symbols and their meaning.

title: Netgear Web Safe Router RP614 v2
description: The Netgear Web Safe Router RP614 v2 provides switching capability among 4 local port cables and a DSL cable. The instruction manual lists these "Key Features". "The RP614 v2 router provides you with multiple Web content filtering options, plus browsing activity reporting and instant alerts -- both via e-mail. Parents and network administrators can establish restricted access policies based on the time-of-day, Website addresses and address keywords, and share high-speed cable/DSL Internet access for up to 253 personal computers. Network Address Translatio (NAT) protects you from hackers. With minimum setup, you can install and use the router within minutes. The RP614 v2 router proves the following features: Easy, web-based setup for installation and management Stateful Packet Inspection, Content Filtering, and Site Blocking Security Built-in 4-port 10/100 Mbps Switch Ethernet connection to a wide area network (WAN) device, such as a cable modem or DSL modem Extensive Protocol Support Login capability Front panel LEDs for easy monitoring of status and activity Flash memory for firmware upgrade"

title: Compaq Evo Computer Setup
description: The CompaqEvo family of personal computers was manufactured in 2002 and included several models, including a "minitower", desktop model, and several "Small Form Factor" models. Once purchased, a user performed several steps, so that the machine could be used. These steps included: Plugging in various cables Installing the operating system (typically a Compaq operating system, Microsoft Windows XP Professional, Microsoft Windows 98, or Microsoft Windows NT) The manuals and disks displayed here support this installation process.

title: Apple Macintosh II Motherboard and Memory Modules
description: This motherboard, dated 1987, provides the backbone for computer processing on a Macintosh II computer. The larger chip (with a gray metal covering) is the Motorola 68020 central processing chip. The smaller chip (with the gray metal covering) is the Motorola 68881 Floating Point Processing chip. The motherboard contains space for six NuBus slots (left) and eight SIMM slots (right). SIMM denotes single in-line memory module. The version here utilizes 30 pins or connections from the memory module to the motherboard. As part of this display, there are four modules of each of the following types. The NEC chips support 256K memory. The TC511000AJL-70 chips, made by Toshiba, contains eight 256K SIMM memory chips. The KM41C1000P chips, made by Apple, also support 1M memory. The NuBus slots allow 32-bit parallel access to the computer bus, following a technology originally developed at MIT. Here three TC511000AJL-70 chips, made by Toshiba, and two KM41C1000P chips, made by Apple, and two are plugged into the SIMM slots.

title: Apple Macintosh II Motherboard and Memory Modules
Apple Macintosh II Motherboard and Memory Modules
Mouse Pads
description: A mouse is a pointing device that provides input for a computer. The mouse detects when it is moved, and transmits changes in position to the computer. The mouse may determine movement in several ways. Mice for the Sun 3/50 (late 1980s) operated by reflecting light off a special mouse pad that contained rows of black dots, arranged in a rectangular grid. The mouse detected when it passed from one dot to the next. By keeping track of direction and the number of dots, the mouse could report changes in its position. Many other mice contain balls that roll as the they are moved. Changes in position can be reported by detecting movement of the ball. This type of mouse requires a reliable surface that will facilitate movement in the ball. Further, a clean surface can help prevent the build up of lint that can affect the ball or its sensors. The mouse on the left illustrates the optical mouse pad of the Sun 3/50, while the mouse pad on the right illustrates a surface with good traction for mice with roller balls.

title: LEDs
description: LEDs or Light Emitting Diodes is a low-power light source, based on semi-conductor technology. As shown here, the lights can be of varying colors.

title: JVC Recordable CD System
description: This JVC Recordable CD System, model BC-TW2000, allowed users to record material on a CD. The equipment was manufactured in April 1994 and has serial number S40400000908. The approach was to connect this equipment to a computer or other device through a cable that utilized the a Small Computer System Interface, or SCSI, protocol. The SCSI cable included here provides the connection to outside equipment. When a SCSI connection was not being used, a SCSI terminator was used to maintain appropriate electrical properties for this machine. A TMS-50PN SCSI terminator is included here. The CD itself was inserted into a tray, and that tray placed in the CD slot at the front of the machine. Connections on the back allow both input from one computer and output to another machine. In addition to the SCSI cable for

title: CheckUPS monitoring software
description: An Uninterruptable Power Supply or UPS provides a battery-based backup for electronic equipment. Under normal operation, the equipment utilizes a normal power cord, plugged into a standard electric outlet, for its electrical power. A UPS is designed to continue that electrical power during times of power failure. To make this work smoothly, normal electrical power must be monitored continually, and the power source switched seamlessly to the UPS when power fails. CheckUPS II was a software suite to maintain this constant monitoring and used at Grinnell's MathLAN with the HP 715 hardware of the mid 1990s. Developed by Best Power, the instruction manual begins, "Best Power CheckUPS II software provides sophisticated UPS monitoring and orderly unattended system shutdown for most popular operating systems." Since this monitoring is designed for various operating systems, the system comes on several media, for installation in a range of machines. The media included here include: A 3 1/4" disk for Unix and Compatible Systems A 5 1/2" floppy disk for Unix and Compatible Systems A CD for DOS, Novell, Windows 3.1, Windows 95, Windows NT, OS/2, and Unix systems A microcasset for use with Hewlett Packard HP-UX systems CheckUPS II also came with two adapter plugs to help connect components. These plugs include 25-pin female plug to 9-pin male plug (labeled INT-0012A) 25-pin male plug to 9-pin female plug (labeled INT-0017A)

title: CentreCom MX20T Twisted Pair Transceiver
description: Computer equipment utilizes a variety of protocols for transmitting data. A common Ethernet cable contains eight wires, follows a 10Base T protocol, and contains an RJ-45 jack at each end. This type of cable is commonly called a "twisted pair cable". A common computer connection utilizes 15 wires or pins and often includes metal shielding to reduce electrical interference. The connector arranges wires into two rows of 8 and 7 wires, called a DA-15 connector. DA-15 sockets are commonly found on the back of computers. The CentreCom MX20T device provides an interface between an RJ-45 jack and a DA-15 connector. Each type of cable is plugged into the device, and the box translates one protocol to the other.

title: Apple ImageWriter II
description: The Apple ImageWriter II was a Dot matrix printer released in September 1985. It utilized a C.Itoh mechanism and was somewhat faster than the original ImageWriter. It was particularly well known for being extremely sturdy. With an optional networking card installed, the ImageWriter II was a low cost alternative to the vastly more expensive LaserWriter. It could also produce basic colour images using a colour ribbon, a feature that was supported by the original version of QuickDraw, which, although it was running on a monochrome hardware platform, actually supported output for eight colors. The ImageWriter II also offered an NLQ (near-letter-quality) ASCII mode, a feature for improved text quality on the Apple II platform or for improved draft printing quality on the Macintosh platform. In 1986 it received the I.D. Design Review and Silver Industrial Design Excellence Awards, making it the first desktop printer to receive such appraisal. ImageWriter II continued in production until late 1996, making it the longest running Apple product in history. Existing models are still useful for printing banners and, like all impact printers, for multi-part forms. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ImageWriter_II

title: Synoptics LattisLink 2800A
description: The LattisLink 2800A, produced by Synoptics, is a preconfigured, standard-based connectivity platform for small, low-density 10BASE-T Ethernet networking environments. The LattisLink 2800A is based on IEEE802.3i 10BASE-T, which specifies the operation of Ethernet at 10 megabits per second (Mb/s) over unshielded twisted pair (UTP) wire. The LattisLink 2800A also offers 16 host ports with standard RJ-45 receptacles for UTP wire connections, and a dedicated Attachment Unit Interface (AUI) interconnect port. You can use the AUI port to connect the Model 2800A to a coaxial cable backbone or to another concentrator using IEEE 802.3 Medium Attachment Unit (MAU) or FOMAUs. Reference: www25.nortelnetworks.com/library/tpubs/pdf/.../893541A.PDF

title: Smith Corona TP0
description: The TP-1 is a micro-processor-controlled daisywheel printer. It was designed to be compatible with most microcomputers now being marketed. The TP-1 is available in two print-format versions: 10-pitch, which gives 105 characters per line or 12-pitch which yields 126 characters per line. A set of control functions is provided to set the operating characteristics of the printer. You can set margin under program control by moving the head of the correct margin position and transmitting a control code to the printer. Reference: InfoWorld - Google Books.

title: VGA/SVGA Distribution Amplifiers
description: The VGA/SVGA Distribution Amplifier is a signal booster and splitter. It allows you to display the same image on multiple monitors from one PC. The built in signal booster enhances the video quality, thus delivering crystal clear picture as if all the monitors were directly connected to the PC. VGA Video Splitter allows you to drive multiple monitors off one PC. These Video Splitters display the same image on all monitors. You can cascade multiple 2, 4, 8 and 16 port splitters to display the same picture or image on up to 90 monitors. Reference: http://www.smartvm.com/Video-Splitters-C1292.htm

title: Fast Ethernet 10/100 Network Interface Card
description: This Network Interface Card is a fast Ethernet card that operates at 10/100 Mbps. It is compliant with PCI version 2.0/2.1 and it is known for reliability and superior performance. Some features of the Ethernet 10/100 Network Interface Card include the Nway auto-negotiation mechanism to automatically detect the network speed and transmission mode, Its support of Windows 95, 98, ME, NT, 2000, XP, NetWare, Linux and Unix and its 32-bit bus master for high throughput and low CPU utilization. Reference: http://www.encore-usa.com/product_item.php?region=us&bid=2&pgid=9&pid=2

title: Apple Disk II Drives
description: The Disk II is Apple's first disk drive. Its Floppy Disk Subsystem was a 5¼-inch floppy disk drive designed by Steve Wozniak and manufactured by Apple Computer. The Disk II was designed specifically for use with the Apple II personal computer family to replace the slower cassette tape storage and cannot be used with any Macintosh computer without an Apple IIe Card. The Disk II uses the Disk II Interface Card to connect to an Apple II computer. Disk II drives are attached directly to the card by a ribbon cable, unlike later generation Apple 5.25-inch drives that use a 19-pin connector. The Disk II Interface Card supports up to two Disk II drives at a time. The Disk II has a sturdy metal case that is painted beige with a black face. It can support a monitor but it is unadvisable to place a monitor on top of two Disk II drives because a heavy monitor can cause the case to bend down and affect the drive mechanism. Reference; http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disk_II#Apple_PC_5.25.22_Drive http://myoldmac.net/SELL/Apple-II-DiskII-Floppy-Drive-A2M0003.htm

title: iOMEGA Predator CD-RW
description: The Predator CD-RW gives cutting edge digital audio and imaging capabilities, as well as great data backup. This stylish, portable device is simple to install and use. The Predator utilizes a fast USB interface with FireWire and PCMCIA. The drive reads up to 6x, writes at 4x, and rewrites at 4x. Utilizing a 2 MB buffer memory, the Predator has quick disk access times of only 125 milliseconds. It is capable of disc-at-once, fixed and variable packet writing, multi-session, and track-at-once methods. The Predator is compatible with both PC and Macintosh computers. Reference: http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/B000056L2R/hasbroidog-20#moreAboutThisProduct

title: LaserWriter Pro User's Guide
description: This guide analyzes the different components of the LaserWriter Pro printer. It also explains installation, troubleshooting and safety measures to the user.

title: 10/100 PCI Network Interface Cards User Guide
description: This guide describes how to install, configure, and troubleshoot the following 3Com Fast EtherLink XL 10/100 PCI interface cards: 3C905B-TX, 3C905B-TX-M, 3C905B-TX-NM, 3C905B-COMBO, 3C905B-FX(SC). Reference: 10/100 PCI NIC User's Guide.

title: Predator CD-RW Quick Install Guide
description: This guide provides information on installing the Predator CD-RW. It also provides the user with safety and troubleshooting informations.

title: Portable StyleWriter Power Adapter (Model M1750)
description: The Portable StyleWriter Power Adapter was manufactured by Apple. It has an input of 120V 25W 60 Hz and an output of 9.5 V 1.5 A.

title: Mouse Pads
description: These 1997 mouse pads were used with mechanical roller ball mice. The HP MathLAN computers of the late 1990s used mice that tracked the movements of a ball mounted in the bottom of each device. For consistent response, the balls required uniform friction. These mouse pads contain a rubber base (keeping the pad in place) and a cloth top (providing a reliable surface for a rubber ball).

title: Sony Turntable PS-LX-150-H
description: Vinyl records and turntables utilized analog technology to record and play back audio. Sound was encoded as variations within the tracks of a phonograph record, and needles sensed these variations. Early records rotated at 78 RPM but fast speeds limited the length of a sound recording. Thus, slower speeds (33 1/3 and 45 RPM) evolved to allow a balance between sound quality and the possible length of a recording. Sony's PS-LX150 Turntable, manufactured about 1995, illustrates the state-of-the-art recording possible at the time. Since motors are subject to vibration and speed fluctuations, a belt drive connected to motor to the turntable. The turntable was relatively heavy, so its momentum would mitigate many variations in speed from the motor. Since a phonograph worked by a stylus rubbing against the tracks in a record, both the phonograph record and the stylus were subject to wear, and wear caused sound quality to deteriorate over time. Thus, turntables used a counterbalanced tone arm, so that the stylus could track the analog signals with minimal pressure. Also, a diamond stylus was preferred as having the longest life. Sideways pressure on a needle and any warping of records could cause distortions in sound. The PS-LX150 uses a long, straight tonearm to minimize these effects. Dust could add to wear, so top-end phonograph players came with a dust cover. Source: Personal experience of Prof. Henry Walker

title: Sony KSP-60 U-matic Cassette
description: U-matic is an analog recording videocassette format first shown by Sony as prototype in October 1969 and released to the market in September 1971. The U-matic tape and video cassette recorder (VCR) were the first cassettes available and among the first video formats to contain the videotape inside a cassette. According to videointerchange.com, U-matic "initially became a standard for early news gathering that was rapidly replacing 16mm film at the time. Image quality and particularly signal to noise ratio was superior to either Vhs or Beta. With only a 3 MHz bandwidth, it was capable of only 240 lines of resolution. Though a color under low-band format and definitely not "hi res", it was comparatively clean and would survive better the multi generation losses incurred during editing — much better than VHS or even Beta could ever hope to match. "With the advent of BetaCam, it was relegated to the higher end industrial marketplace. Using a quality verses price metric, the 3/4 U format was perhaps the most successful format of all time." References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Videotape http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U-matic http://www.videointerchange.com/video-history.htm

title: Sony PDP-64 Pro Digital Audio Tape Plus
description: Digital Audio Tape (DAT or R-DAT) resembles a compact audio cassette, but the DAT cassette is about half the size, uses digital technology rather than analog, and utilizes only one side in recording rather than two. The DAT format was introduced Sony in 1987, and it provided excellent sound quality with very little noise. However, the format was relatively expensive and this limited its widespread use. Reference: http://4audiotapes.com/the-digital-audio-tape-history.html

title: Sony PDP-64 Pro Digital Audio Tape Plus
Sony PDP-64 Pro Digital Audio Tape Plus
VHS Cassette
description: Although video tape systems had been developed as early as 1956, the first systems were expensive and not readily available. As a response, JVC Corporation developed VHS (Video Home System) cassettes and VCR recorders about 1975 as an inexpensive means to store video materials for home use. The early systems could record programs lasting about two hours. Later systems could play VHS tape at several speeds, trading quality with the length of the session. For example, a typical cassette (labeled E-120) contained 173.7 meters (570 feet) of tape, and could record 83 minutes, 120 minutes, or 258 minutes of material, depending upon the speed selected. Generally the maximum length for a VHS cassette tape (labeled T-210) was 433.1 meters (1,421 feet) at the lowest acceptable tape thickness. Reference: http://www.ehow.com/about_5045616_history-vhs-vcrs.html

title: LaserDisc
description: A LaserDisc (LD) uses small pits on a plastic-coated disk to store digital data. A laser reads the pits by scanning the disc's surface, and this approach provides the basis for new technologies, such as CDs and DVDs. inventors.about.com provides this history: "David Paul Gregg first envisioned the optical disk (or VIDEODISK as he named it) in 1958 and patented the technology in 1961 and 1969. Gregg's company Gauss Electrophysics was acquired by MCA in the early 1960s. MCA also bought the patent rights for the optical disk which included a the process for making a video record disc and other optical disk technology. In 1978, MCA Discovision released the first consumer Optical disk player in Atlanta, Georgia." Although LaserDiscs provided high-quality video with two analog audio tracks, the format experienced limited popularity. LaserDiscs were reasonably popular in Japan, Hong Kong, and parts of southeast Asia. However, title selection and high costs limited the adoption of LaserDiscs in North America, Europe, and Australia. References: http://inventors.about.com/od/ofamousinventions/a/Optical_Disk.htm http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laserdisc

title: Shuttle X27D Computer
description: This computer was purchased in 2008 as part of an exploration of inexpensive options for MathLAN. The computer cost about $200.00 and ran a version of Linux. The X27D was capable of running all software in use on MathLAN at the time. However, compared to MathLAN workstations of its day (HP dc5700 Microtowers), the Shuttle was slow and occasionally seemed underpowered.

title: Gateway 2000 NOMAD 425DXL
description: The Gateway Nomad 425DXL is a 1992 vintage laptop computer, and as such does not have sufficient resources to run recent Microsoft Windows software. The hardware is quite reliable, and thus it has seemed reasonable to use it as a Linux system because Linux has the advantage of taking advantage of the full 32-bit capabilities of such a small system, without requiring large amounts of disk space or memory. Also included with this laptop is a mouse because the laptop does not have a touch-pad. It also has a user's guide which provides information on installation and configuration of the laptop.

title: DELL Inspiron 3200
description: DELL Inspiron 3200 laptop has a microprocessor speed of 266 MHz and an internal and external cache memory of 32 KB and 512-KB respectively. It also has a maximum RAM of 144MB. It uses a Sound Blaster Pro-compatible voice and music function audio type. Reference: http://support.dell.com/support/edocs/systems/ptwist/specs.htm

title: 10/100 PCI Fast Ethernet Adapter
description: This Fast Ethernet Desktop PCI Adapter includes advanced features such as Wake-on-LAN and IEEE 802.3x support. Computers and operating systems that support Advanced Configuration and Power Interface (ACPI) can remotely wake your PC from anywhere on the network with the Wake-on-LAN feature. With support for 802.3x flow control, data is received and transmitted smoothly to and from the network at a rate of 100Mbps. Included with the Adapter is a User's Manual and a Diskette. The Manual provides the user with installing and troubleshooting information. The Diskette contains the Ethernet Adapter's setup program for configuring the device. Reference: http://shopper.cnet.com/adapters-nics/startech-10-100-pci/4014-3380_9-1742496.html

title: CREATIVE Sound Blaster 16PCI
description: Sound Blaster 16 is a series of add-on sound cards from Creative Technology. Sound Blaster 16, invented in June 1992, was the successor to Sound Blaster Pro and introduced 16-bit digital audio sampling to the Sound Blaster line. SB16 also added an expansion-header for add-on MIDI-daughter-boards with sample-based synthesis capabilities complying to the General MIDI standard, a socket for an optional digital signal processor dubbed the Advanced Signal Processor (ASP, or later CSP), and an MPU-401 compatible UART for communication with external MIDI-devices. The Sound Blaster 16PCI card comes with a Getting Started Manual which provides installation and troubleshooting information for the user. It also comes with a Software Pack (in CD-ROM format) and Drivers for Windows 95, 98, NT 4.0 and Windows 2000. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound_Blaster_15

title: IOMEGA Zip CD 650
description: The ZIP CD is a USB CD burner produced by Iomega in the late 1990's. It has CD-R AND CD-RW recording support. It operates at a fixed recording speed of 4x and a reading speed of 6x. It also uses the Adaptec Easy CD Creator Deluxe 3.5c for Iomega. When the ZipCD 650 was produced it was able to record onto 700MB CDs but could only burn data up to 650MB. There was a 3rd party firmware that forced the ZipCD 650 to be able to write data CDs up to 700MB but this made the drive unstable. Formerly, Iomega used the DirectCD software from Adaptec to allow UDF drive-letter access to CD-R or CD-RW media but Iomega's recent reliability tests indicated that undetected data errors may occur when ZipCD USB Drive is used with with DirectCD application. As a result of this problem, user's have been advised not to use DirectCD application, but rather use Adaptec Easy CD Creator application. Reference: http://www.activewin.com/reviews/hardware/zip/cd650usb/index.shtml

title: OKIDATA MICROLINE 192 Plus
description: The Okidata Microline 192 has a speed draft of up to 435cps, a near letter quality speed of 75cps and a maximum graphics resolution (dpi) of 240 x 216. It also uses a standard parallel interface. The Okidata Microline can print the following internal fonts in normal and bold: Courier 17, 12, 10, 6, and 5 cpi. The Microline prints internal fonts in bold by double-striking the same character.

title: DAT Tapes Box
description: Digital Audio Tape (DAT or R-DAT) is a signal recording and playback medium developed by Sony and introduced in 1987.[1] In appearance it is similar to a compact audio cassette, using 4 mm magnetic tape enclosed in a protective shell, but is roughly half the size at 73 mm × 54 mm × 10.5 mm. As the name suggests, the recording is digital rather than analog. DAT has the ability to record at higher, equal or lower sampling rates than a CD (48, 44.1 or 32 kHz sampling rate respectively) at 16 bits quantization. If a digital source is copied then the DAT will produce an exact clone, unlike other digital media such as Digital Compact Cassette or non-Hi-MD MiniDisc, both of which use lossy data compression. Like most formats of videocassette, a DAT cassette may only be recorded on one side, unlike an analog compact audio cassette. Also included in this box is a Quantum DLT CleaningTape III used for cleaning DAT Tapes, and Verbatim 5 1/4" Minidisks for storage.

title: TANDY 102 Portable Computer
description: The TANDY 102 was manufactured in 1986 to replace TANDY 100 which is considered the world's first laptop computers. It has a full-size keyboard. It also uses Microsoft BASIC programming language, word processing software, telecommunications software and a built-in 300 baud modem. This particular Tandy 102 Computer with external disk-drive was purchased by Beryl Clotfelter in 1987 for use in Grinnell-in-London. The GIL site had a printer to which the Tandy could be connected, and class hand-outs were prepared on the computer and then printed at the GIL office. Beryl also used this forerunner of the laptop computer for keeping notes while traveling. The Tandy was immensly popular with journalists. They were able to write out their articles on the road on the Tandy's excellent keyboard, the through the 300 baud modem, send their articles through to the newspapers mainframe. Reference: http://the-gadgeteer.com/2004/03/29/radio_shack_trs_80_portable_computer_review/

title: TANDY Portable Disk Drive 1
description: The TANDY Portable Disk Drive 2 was manufactured in 1985. It has a 3.5" disk drive and runs up to 10 hours on 4 AA cells or (much longer) with AC adapter. Connects to Model 100 serial port. Provides 100K of external storage for less than 2 pounds of weight. With utilities disk and manual. Uses standard 720K 3.5" floppy disks; format is single side, 40 tracks, 2 sectors per track, 1280 bytes per sector. In short, Incompatible with just about everything. Just as the TANDY 102 Portable was purchased Beryl Clotfelter in 1987 for use in Grinnell-in-London, this was also used in GIL. This portable Disk Drive comes along with a Disk Box which contains an Utility Diskette Copy and other 3 1/2 " Floppy disks. It also comes with a serial Cable and an AC Adapter for power supply. Reference: http://sliderule.mraiow.com/wiki/Tandy_Radio_Shack_TRS-80_Model_100/Tandy_Portable_Disk_Drive

title: TANDY 102 Owner's Manual
description: This manual teaches the user how to use the Tandy 102 Portable Computer. It explains how to write notes and letters, how to cut and paste, how to operate an address book, how to dial telephone numbers and how to write BASIC Programs. It also provides safety and troubleshooting informations.

title: TS-DOS Manual
description: Users of Tandy and NEC notebook computers were once faced with a dilemma: limited room for files and no efficient way of storing and retrieving them. Files could be stored only on cassettes - a slow and unreliable method. TS-DOS was manufactured by Traveling Software to allow notebook computer users store their data directly on an IBM-compatible PC, greatly increasing storage size and ease of use. This manual simply explains how to use TS-DOS, how to install it, how to run it and how to use it with TEXT and BASIC. Included with this manual is a 3 1/2 " floppy disk which also explains how TS-DOS can be used, Reference: TS-DOS Manual

title: LAPDOS User's Manual
description: LAPDOS is a disk-management system created by Traveling Software as a response to the demand for a direct link between PC computers, with their large storage capacities, and the Tandy and NEC notebook computers, with their unequaled portability. In its original form LAPDOS links PCs to notebook computers indirectly, through the portable disk drive. The new component makes the portable disk drive unnecessary. The new component of LAPDOS consists of a small BASIC program that runs on a notebook computer and makes it act like a portable disk drive. Once you have started that program, you run the original LAPDOS program on a PC and remain at the PC to control the process of transferring files and performing the other file-management operations the program makes possible. The notebook computer responds to commands from the PC much like a portable disk drive. Though LAPDOS operated almost identically on the notebook computer as it does on the portable disk drive, the connections-PC to portable disk drive and PC to notebook computer - are distinct and not interchangeable. This Manual simply explains how LAPDOS can be used efficiently on portable computers. It provides information on installing LAPDOS, on Setup Commands, on Exchanging Data and dealing with Problems and Error Messages while using LAPDOS. Also packaged with this manual are three 3 1/2 " floppy disks and two 5 1/4 " floppy disks which all explain how to use LAPDOS.

title: TANDY 102 Applications and BASIC Reference Guide
description: This Guide explains how BASIC is implemented on the Tandy 102. It explains the BASIC operations, BASIC data, BASIC Input/Output, BASIC files, BASIC Keywords and other BASIC tools. Included with this manual is a TANDY Utilities Diskette and ULTIMATE ROM II SAMPLE FILES Diskette which is also for the TANDY.

title: ROM-View 80 Manual
description: ROM-View 80 is a display enhancement program that expands the 40-column screen capacity of the Tandy Model 100 and the NEC PC-8201. ROM-View 80 allows the computer to display 8 lines by 60-columns at once, and scroll up to 8 lines by 80-columns which makes the Tandy Computer more efficient. This manual explains the benefits of using ROM-VIEW 80. It also explains how to use the various applications that come with ROM-View 80 and these includes: TEXT-80, BASIC-80, TELCOM-80, and other applications.

title: T-BASE Manual
description: T-Base is a Database Manager for the TRS-80 Model 100/200, the Olivetti M10 and the NEC PC-8201. This manual explains the various functions of T-BASE. It also explains how T-BASE and its corresponding programs are operated.

title: T-WORD Manual
description: TWORD is a ROM-based Word Processor For the Tandy 100/200 and the NEC PC-8201. This Manual explains how to use T-WORD. It explains how to use the Print Menu and Print Commands. It also highlights Merging and Word Processing within TWORD.

title: TANDY Portable Disk Drive 2 Operation Manual
description: This manual explains the installation, configuration and initialization of the Portable Disk Drive 2, It further explains battery Installation and power source. It goes ahead to explain file management and the various commands it features.

title: IDEA Manual
description: IDEA is a Word Processing Program created in 1984 for Portable Computers such as TRS-80 Model 100/200, OLIVETTI M-10 and NEC PC-8201. This manual explains how IDEA operates on the Ultimate ROM II. It also explains basic an advanced uses OF IDEA on the portable computer.

title: The Ultimate ROM II Installation Guide and Quick Reference
description: The Ultimate ROM II is a chip that is a combination of various programs such as T-Word, IDEA, T-Base, ROM-View 80 and TS-DOS. This Manual explains the Installation and Initialization process of the Ultimate ROM II chip. It also explains how the user can run the various programs on the chip.

title: LAPDOS User's Manual
description: LAPDOS is a disk-management system created by Traveling Software as a response to the demand for a direct link between PC computers, with their large storage capacities, and the Tandy and NEC notebook computers, with their unequaled portability. In its original form LAPDOS links PCs to notebook computers indirectly, through the portable disk drive. The new component makes the portable disk drive unnecessary. The new component of LAPDOS consists of a small BASIC program that runs on a notebook computer and makes it act like a portable disk drive. Once you have started that program, you run the original LAPDOS program on a PC and remain at the PC to control the process of transferring files and performing the other file-management operations the program makes possible. The notebook computer responds to commands from the PC much like a portable disk drive. Though LAPDOS operated almost identically on the notebook computer as it does on the portable disk drive, the connections-PC to portable disk drive and PC to notebook computer - are distinct and not interchangeable. This Manual simply explains how LAPDOS can be used efficiently on portable computers. It provides information on installing LAPDOS, on Setup Commands, on Exchanging Data and dealing with Problems and Error Messages while using LAPDOS. Also packaged with this manual are three 3 1/2 " floppy disks and two 5 1/4 " floppy disks which all explain how to use LAPDOS.

title: EPSON LX-800 User's Manual
description: Epson LX-800 is a printer. This Manual simply explains installing and using the Epson printer.

title: Zenith Data Systems User's Guide
description: This user guide provides the user with safety and troubleshooting informations about the MastersPort II. It tells the user how to setup the laptop and connect peripherals. It also provides in-depth information about the battery pack and the floppy disk drive. Included in the user's guide is an MS-DOS systems disk, in form of a 1.44MB diskette.

title: MAGNAVOX Computer Monitor 80
description: This Monitor is compatible with Apple, Atari, Commodore, IBM-PC, Texas Instr 1199/4A and Timex Sinclair 2068.

title: PartitionMagic User's Guide
description: PartitionMagic is a program that allows the user to be able to secure data by physically separating it from other files. Separate partitions also make backups to networks and removable drives easy. PartitionMagic also helps the user reliably run multiple operating systems on the same computer. PartitionMagic includes Bootmagic, a powerful boot manager that helps the user to safely install new operating systems and choose which operating system to use when starting the computer. This manual provides information about installing and running PartitionMagic. It also contains safety and troubleshooting informations.

title: HP-16C Owner's Handbook
description: This guide teaches the user how to use the HP 16-C calculator. It explains binary and hexadecimal conversions. It also explains Arithmetic and Bit Manipulation Functions as well as floating-point numbers. It also emphasizes memory and storage.

title: Apple Color LaserWriter Transparencies
description: The Apple Color LaserWriter 12/660 PS is a high-performance, multi-platform Adobe PostScript color laser printer that provides outstanding results for both color and gray-scale printing. A pack contains 50 letter-size sheets. The Color LaserWriter 12/660 PS provides a breakthrough in color printing performance. With its 600-dot-per-inch resolution, it provides the outstanding print quality you need-fast. Its performance contributes to your efficiency; for example, the Color LaserWriter 12/660 PS can be used to print multiple color copies (up to 180 per hour), making it perfect for producing handouts. Reference; http://docs.info.apple.com/article.html?artnum=20484&coll=ap

title: Panasonic RQ-CR07V
description: The Panasonic RQ-CR07V is an AM/FM Cassette Player with a Digital Tuner type and an LCD Display type. Connected to it is a Stereo Headphone used to listen to what is being played by the Player. It also has an auto reverse playback and uses 2 x AA batteries to operate. Reference: http://www.dealtime.com/xPF-Panasonic-RQ-CR07V

title: Verbatim DataLife 5 1/4" Diskettes
description: hese 5 1/4 diskettes also called Minidisks are double-sided and double-density. This Verbatim brand contains 10 pieces of this kind of diskettes.

title: HP-HIL ID MODULE
description: The unit is a Hewlett Packard HP-HIL ID Module 46084A. It has the older style HP keyboard and mouse interface ports. It is a computer bus used by Hewlett-Packard to connect keyboards, mice, trackballs, digitizers, tablets, bar-code readers, rotary knobs, touch-screens, and other human interface peripherals to their HP 9000 workstations. The bus was in use until the mid 1990s, when HP substituted PS/2 technology for HIL. The PS/2 peripherals were themselves replaced with USB-connected models. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP-HIL

title: ANDREA NC-8
description: Andrea’s ultra-lightweight, head-mounted, NC-8 noise canceling microphone offers a unique design that allows users to easily position the soft-flex microphone boom for optimum voice input. The NC-8 rests comfortably on your head without covering your ears so you are free to hear rich sound from your multimedia speakers. Noise Cancellation technology increases voice intelligibility and speech recognition accuracy, particularly in noisy environments. Reference: http://bodylink.com.au/ProductHTMLS/audio_devices/NC%208.htm

title: Microcom AX/2400 MNP 4
description: Microcom, Inc. was a major modem vendor during the 1980s, although they were never as popular as the "big three", Hayes, U.S. Robotics (USR) and Telebit. Nevertheless they hold an important place in modem history due to their introduction of the MNP error-correction and compression protocols, which were widely used under license by most modem manufacturers in the 1990s. Compaq purchased the company in 1997. MNP 4 was a further improvement on MNP 3, adding a variable packet size system they referred to as Adaptive Packet Assembly. With MNP 4 operation, the two modems constantly monitor the line for dropped packets, and if a certain threshold is crossed (selected by the user), the modem "drops back" to a smaller packet size. This means that when a packet is dropped, the amount of data that has to be re-sent is smaller, leading to better throughput. MNP 4 also introduced Data Phase Optimization, a simple change to the protocol that allowed some of the packet-framing information to be dropped after the link was set up, further reducing protocol overhead. The combination of these features, along with MNP 3's lack of byte-framing, allowed for a further increase in throughput efficiency. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microcom_Networking_Protocol#MNP_4

title: MICROCOM Plug-In Transformer
description: This transformer normally used with MICROCOM has an input supply of 120V 60Hz 35W and an output supply of 19.5V 0.8AMP.

title: Four-Color Ribbon for Apple ImageWriter II Printers
description: The four-color ribbon comprises of four inks used in printing: three secondary colors plus black. These ink colors are cyan, magenta and yellow; abbreviated as CMYK. Cyan can be thought of as minus-red, magenta as minus-green, and yellow as minus-blue. These inks are semi-transparent or translucent. Where two such inks overlap on the paper due to sequential printing impressions, a primary color is perceived. For example, yellow (minus-blue) overprinted by magenta (minus green) yields red. Where all three inks may overlap, almost all incident light is absorbed or subtracted, yielding near black. It is because of this poor "subtractive" black that a separate black ink is used. The secondary or subtractive colors cyan, magenta and yellow may be considered "primary" by printers and watercolorists (whose basic inks and paints are transparent). Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_printing

title: T437-4NB Printer Ribbon for Apple ImageWriter I & II
description: This printer ribbon is used with Apple ImageWriter I & II as well as C.ITOH ProWriter 8500 & 9500 Series. It is a one color (black only) Printer Ribbon. When this ribbon is used, the printer prints document in black and white only.

title: HP Inkjet print Cartridge (51626A)
description: This black inkjet cartridge has a maximum yield of 794 pages. It can be used with HP DesJet Printers, DeskWriter Printers, DesignJet Printers, OfficeJet Printers and even HP Fax Machines. Reference: http://www.4inkjets.com/HP/Remanufactured/Ink-Cartridge/Black/51626A/407-Product.html

title: SHARP EL-520L
description: The EL-520L is designed for all-round energy-saving operation. It saves the energy of the student with its ease of operation. And it saves battery power by using solar energy. With 192 scientific functions, it easily solves all problems for senior pupils. The results are easy to read from the two-line display. And if you do occasionally make an incorrect entry, then this is indicated by the 3 error messages. Reference: http://www.sharp-eu.com/katalog/isd/calculator/el520l.htm

title: SHARP EL-506P
description: The Sharp EL-506P scientific calculator was introduced in 1984. It has 56 functions available and 42 keys. It uses an LCD display type. Reference: http://mycalcdb.free.fr/main.php?l=0&id=1615

title: HP 16C Calculator
description: The hp 16C calculator was introduced in 1982 and discontinued in 1989. The HP-16C is a computer programmer's calculator, designed to assist in debugging. It can display numbers in hexadecimal, decimal, octal, and binary and convert numbers from one base to the other. To accommodate long binary numbers, the display can be 'windowed' by shifting it left and right. For consistency with the computer the programmer is working with, the word size can be set to different values from 1 to 64 bits. Binary-arithmetic operations can be performed as unsigned, 1's complement, or 2's complement operations. This allows the calculator to emulate the programmer's computer. A number of specialized functions are provided to assist the programmer, including left- and right shifting, masking, and bitwise logical operations. Reference: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP-16C#The_HP-16C

title: AX MICROCOM Owner's Guide
description: This Owner's guide contains a Quick Reference Card for the AX Modem, which explains configuration of the port to the user. It also contains a Quick Installation Guide which tells the user how to install the Modem, Included in the package is a 5 1/4" Diskette which also explains safety and troubleshooting precautions to the user.

title: Proxima DESKTOP PROJECTOR 6860 User's Guide and Safety Instruction
description: This User's Guide provides the user with information about the Proxima Desktop Projector. It explains the installation process of the projector as well as its maintenance, It also explains its various operations such as power on and off, and others. The safety instructions manual simply tells the user what not to do with the projector. It is easy to read and convenient because the manual is written in seven different languages which includes German, French, Spanish and Portuguese.

title: PROXIMA Liquid Crystal Projector DP5800 User's Guide.
description: This User's Guide provides information about the Proxima LCD Projector Model DP5800. It explains how to install the projector and also how to configure it.

title: hp ScanJet 4c Scanner Installation Guide
description: The hp ScanJet 4c scanner is a versatile scanner that captures black and white and color drawings, photographs, and text at high resolution. The hp ScanJet 4c scanner includes HPDeskScan II scanning software for scanning images, Optical Character Recognition (OCR) software, for scanning text into a format that can be edited, Image Editing software for modifying the images you scan, and Visioneer PaperPort software for distributing scanned documents in a variety of formats. The optional Automatic Document Feeder (ADF) lets you automatically scan multiple pages. This manual provides information on how to install and configure the hp ScanJet 4c Scanner. Reference: hp ScanJet Installation Guide.

title: PIRELLI OPTICAL CABLE
description: Formerly in Grinnell College, copper cables were used to connect all the computers together for internet purposes. This proved to be a bad method because whenever there was a thunder-storm, the cable carried the electric charges from lightning and as a result of this, the motherboard burnt. In order to better the connection process, optical cables were used because they did not carry electric charges the way copper cables did.

title: Apple DiskWare
description: This contains various Apple Softwares such as APPLE PASCAL on 5 1/4 " floppy disks.

title: Parallel Printer Interface Card Installation and Operation Manual
description: The parallel printer interface card allows the Apple II to produce hard-copy (printed) output on a wide variety of printers. This compact board not only provides a complete electronic link between the printer and the Apple II motherboard, but in addition it contains a powerful firmware package to handle interpretation of program commands for the printer, compensation for varying printer line lengths and special control characters peculiar to a given printer. This manual includes information about installing and configuring the interface cards.

title: AppleSoft II Basic Programming Reference Manual
description: This manual provides information about programming in AppleSoft.

title: MicroWare HandBook.
description: Microware disks come in two types: program disks and data disks. A program disk will run by itself and does not need anythign extra to make it work, but a data disk will no run by itself, it usually needs a high-level language such as Pascal and BASIC. This manual provides information on everything about using MicroWare disks, from installing to maintaining.

title: MICROCOMPUTER Problem Solving Using PASCAL
description: This book was designed both for introductory courses in computer problem solving, at the freshman and sophomore college level as well as individual self study. It contains information about programming with PASCAL.

title: Apple In-Depth Manual
description: This manual provides information about Apple Products. It includes information about Apple's Applications Software, Apple's languages and Utilities which include Apple pascal, Apple PILOT, Apple FORTRAN and Apple BASIC. This manual also provides information about Apple Operating Systems, Apple Accessories and Apple Interface Cards.

title: VISCALC Computer Software Program User's Manual
description: This manual shows how to use Visicalc and the Apple computer to solve problems in professional work and everyday life. VisiCalc gives the user an easy way to use a personal computer to solve problems without having to spend time programming.

title: APPLE II Reference Manual
description: This manual provides the user with information about using the Apple II computer. It explains memory organization, input/output structure and hardware configuration.

title: The APPLESOFT TUTORIAL
description: This manual provides information about programming in Apple's Applesoft BASIC. This manual also provides information about installing the Apple computer and configuring it.

title: PASCAL User Manual and Report
description: This manual is the second edition written by kathleen Jensen. It provides information about programming in PASCAL.

title: Apple Language System Installation and Operating Manual
description: The Apple Language System greatly enhances the power of the Apple II Computer because it gives a full 64 kilobytes of random-access memory (RAM), as much as the microprocessor can address directly. With the Apple Language Sysytem, the user can operate the computer directly in Integer BASIC, Applesoft BASIC, and Pascal. The heart of the Language System is the language Card, which adds 16K to the apple's 48K of RAM. In addition, it includes the Autostart ROM IC (Integrated Circuit), which facilitates screen editing of BASIC programs and automatically starts your disk drive when you turn on the computer. This manual provides more information about installing, configuring and maintaining the Apple Language System Reference: Apple language System Guide.

title: Introductory Programs For The Apple II Plus
description: This booklet contains documentation for nine programs that came with the Apple II Plus system. The programs illustrates some of the capabilities available to the user with the Apple II Plus.

title: Introduction To Applesoft BASIC Student Text.
description: This student manual introduces elementary and intermediate level topics of the AppleSoft BASIC programming language for the Apple II microcomputer. It was created for use in a classroom lecture setting with assignments given to students. One of the greates advantages of this text is that no prior programming knowledge is required on the part of the students using the manual.

title: The Creative Apple Manual
description: This manual provides the reader about information of the wide range of applications possible with Apple. It explains Graphics, Music composing, Word Processing, and other benefits of using Apple.

title: SANYO 9-IN Diagonal Video Monitor Owner's Manual
description: This guide provides information about the identification and operation of controls in the 9-in Diagonal Video Monitor. It also provides valuable information on safety precautions.

title: APPLE PASCAL Reference Manual
description: This book describes the Apple UCSD Pascal system, which incorporates UCSD Pascal and several Apple extensions for graphics and other uses. The Apple Pascal system runs on the Apple II and Apple II-Plus computers. While the system was primarily intended to use the Apple keyboard and the usual TV or monitor, an external CRT terminal such as the Soroc IQ 120 can act as the CONSOLE device, connected to the Apple through an Apple communications Interface Card. With such an external terminal, it becomes possible to do text and program editing in upper and lower case on a large (80 characters by 24 lines) screen. This manual provides more information about APPLE PASCAL System as well as the various functions it entails.

title: Setting Up Your Studio Display
description: This manual provides information about installing and configuring the Apple Studio Display.

title: Setting Up Your Power Macintosh G3
description: This manual includes setup and expansion information for Power Macintosh G3 and Macintosh Server G3 computers.

title: Minisette-20 Owner's Manual
description: Minisette-20 is a voice actuated compact cassette recorder. This manual provides information about Minisette-20. Information such as: power supply, recording and other tape tips.

title: HP DeskJet 695C/697C Series Printer Setup and User's Guide
description: The setup guide provides information about installing the printer, inserting print cartridges and installing the printer software. The User's Guide provides information about printing on different kinds of paper, and how the printer can be used appropriately. These guides are accompanied by a CD-ROM which also includes information about using HP DeskJet 690 Series Printer.

title: Troubleshooting Handbook for MAC 0S 8.5
description: This manual includes information on problem-solving, hard disk repair, and software installation of MAC OS 8.5.

title: intel 8080 and intel MCS-80 User's Manual
description: The 8080 is a complete 8-bit parallel, central processor unit (CPU) for use in general purpose digital computer systems. It was fabricated on a single LSI chip using Intel's n-channel silicon gate MOS process. The 8080 transfers data and internal state information via an 8-bit, directional 3-state Data Bus. This manual provides more information about intel 8080. It also provides information about installing and configuring intel 8080. Reference: Intel 8080 User's Manual.

title: TRS-80 Computer Comic Book
description: This comic book which is titled "The Computer Trap" was created by RadioShack. It is a comic that explains the TRS-80 Computer and how it was used.

title: TANDY Computer Comic Book
description: This comic book which is titled, "The Computers that said NO to Drugs", was created by RadioShack. It explains how the Tandy was used in various settings.

title: hp Color LaserJet 4500, 4500N, 4500DN Printer Reference Guide
description: This manual provides more information about installing and configuring the ho Color LaserJet printers. It also includes safety and maintenance information.

title: Transistorized Power Supply Model EUW-17
description: The Model EUW-17 Power Supply was designed to supply low operating voltages and currents. Its variable output voltage and compact size make it ideal for operating experimental transistor circuits, both in the laboratory and in the classroom. This manual provides information about using the Transistor.

title: HEATHKIT Variable Voltage Regulated Power Supply Manual
description: This manual explains the construction, installation and operation of the Heathkit voltage power supply.

title: HEATHKIT Vacuum Tube Voltmeter Manual
description: The Heathkit model V-7A VTVM represents the first kit instrument using a pre-wired, prefabricated printed circuit board to its design. This manual provides more information about unpacking, installing and operating the HeathKit Vacuum Tube Voltmeter.

title: HEATHKIT Sine-Square Wave Generator Manual
description: The Heathkit Model IG-82 Sine Square Wave Generator is designed for servicing and testing audio equipment, radio and television receivers, and recording equipment. Its wide frequency range and high quality waveforms also make it ideal for many general laboratory and industrial applications. This manual provides additional information about the Sine-Square Wave Generator. It also explains how to assemble and operate the Sine-Square Wave Generator.

title: HEATHKIT Decade Condenser
description: This manual provides information about constructing and using the Heathkit Model DC-1 Decade Condenser.

title: HEATHKIT Laboratory 5" Oscilloscope Manual
description: The heathkit Model IO-12 Oscilloscope is a wide-range, general-purpose oscilloscope. This manual provides information on assembling and operating the Oscilloscope.

title: HEATHKIT Isolation Transformer Manual
description: The Model IP-10 Isolation Transformer provides isolation between the AC power line and any equipment that is plugged into one of its output receptacles. This practically eliminates AC shock hazards when testing AC-DC and transformerless equipments. This manual provides more information about assembling and operating the Isolation Transformer.

title: HEATHKIT Battery Eliminator
description: The Heathkit Battery Eliminator Model BE-5, incorporates in its design a low-ripple filter section. This increases the usefulness of the unit in that it may now, along with its many other applications, be used in servicing Hybrid car radios and other devices incorporating space charge tubes and transistors. This manual provides more information on assembling and operating the Heathkit Battery Eliminator.

title: HEATHKIT RF SIGNAL GENERATOR Manual
description: The Heath Model IGW-102S RF Signal Generator is an accurate and stable source of modulated or unmodulated RF (radio frequency) signals. This manual provides information about assembling and operating the RF Signal Generator.

title: HEATHKIT A.C VOLTMETER
description: This manual provides information on how to assemble and use the Heathkit A.C Voltmeter.

title: Macintosh Programmer's Workshop Assembler
description: This Manual contains information about using the Macintosh Programmer's Workshop Assembler. It explains syntax, macro variables and commands on the assembler.

title: Macintosh Programmer's workshop Development Environment, Volume II
description: This manual is a command dictionary that describes each of the tools, scripts, and built-in commands of the Macintosh Programmer's Workshop 3.0.

title: MacsBug 6.1 Reference
description: MacsBug is a Motorola 68000-family assembly-language debugger customized for the entire Macintosh family of computers. MacsBug, which was first introduced in 1981, has continued to evolve along with the Macintosh. MacsBug 6.1 runs on the Macintosh Plus, Macintosh SE, and Macintosh II, and supports all members of the 68000 family. It handles the MC68881 floating-point co-processor and the MC68851 Memory Management Unit (MMU). It also supports external display on the Macintosh. This manual includes more information about MacsBug as well as information on using MacsBug as a debugger. Reference: MacsBug Manual

title: Apple Joystick Controller
description: These are the early joysticks usually connected to the Apple Computers.

title: SHARP PC-3010
description: Sharp built this notebook computer in about 1995. Costing about $2500, this machine used an Intel 80486 processor that ran at 66 MHz. 4MB of main memory were standard in this unit, but the memory could be expanded to 8MB ($499) or 16MB ($899). The notebook was shipped with MS-DOS 6.22. This computer utilized a full-sized keyboard and a touch-pad device, which was quite new at the time. It also has a 3 1/2" floppy disk drive.

title: Sytek Localnet 20/199
description: This unit checks to see whether there are valid connections between the inputs. The producer, Sytec Inc., now known as Hughes LAN Systems, Inc., created the NetBIOS protocol. This protocol allows applications on several computers to communicate over a local area network.

title: Tandy 4000
description: The Tandy 4000 was introduced in 1987 by the Radio Shack Corporation. This computer was mostly IBM-compatible, so that many programs written for an IBM PC could be run on the Tandy 4000. The Tandy 400 used the Intel 80386 processing chip, and a standard configuration came with 2 megabytes of 80-nanosecond RAM. Its listed price was $3799.

title: Tandy 1000 TL/2
description: The Tandy 1000 TL/2, available from Radio Shack Corporation in about 1986, used the Intel 80286 processor running at 8 MHz. The computer came with 640 kilobytes of main memory, and users could expand this by 128 kilobytes (but this extra memory often was used specifically with a video graphics card). Various enthusiasts touted the machines "3-voice" sound card and its EGA graphics. DOS-3 with Windows 2.03 provided a standard operating-system environment.

title: Tandy Enhanced Keyboard (Grant O. Gale Observatory)
description: The Tandy Enhanced Keyboard, developed in the mid 1980s, provided a standard typewriter keyboard (on left), numeric key pad (on right), and function keys (top). The cord utilizes a 5-pin connector that fit computers from many manufacturers at the time.

title: Turbo Pascal Reference Manual Version 2.0
description: A softcover manual that gives information on programming using the Pascal programming language. It is a handy reference manual for both the experienced programmer who needs a reminder of function syntax, or for the amateur programmer with little experience. Copyrighted 1983 by Borland International Incorporated.

title: Pschologram (light box)
description: The image is a visualization of mathematics, a juxtaposition of related fractals. The image was made by taking 9 photographic views of a 2D image from different viewpoints, electronically cutting them into strips and repasting them. A barrier screen is placed on the light box in front of the recomposed image in such a way that each eye sees a different view, making it appear 3D, and as you look at it from different viewpoints, you see a slightly different 3D image. (Description by Judy Brown)

title: User's Guide for COMPAQ: Introducing Microsoft Windows 98
description: Manual accompanying purchase of a Microsoft Windows 95 operating system. Gives basic information on the parameters and abilities of the operating system, as well as how to use the general features.

title: VHS-C Cassette
description: VHS-C was a compact version of the regular VHS format introduced in 1982 and used primarily for consumer-grade compact analog recording camcorders.

title: Commodore 1541-II Disk Drive
description: Commodore 1541-II disk drive contains its own built-in microcomputer to control its various operations, along with enough ROM and RAM memory to operate without any help from the computer. Commodore's DOS (Disk Operating System) is installed in the disk drive and does not require any internal memory in the computer to do its work, nor does it have to be loaded before use like DOS on other computers. The Commodore 1541 is completely independent that once it begins working on a command, it will complete it while the computer goes on to other tasks, thereby allowing the user to do two things at once. The Commodore 1541 also communicates with the computer and other devices over a cable and interface known as the Commodore Serial Bus. Commodore 1541 comes with a user's guide which explains various functions of the Commodore, as well as maintenace and safety precautions to the user. Reference: Commodore 1541-II user's guide.

title: Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
description: Manufactured in the early 2000s, the Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer advertised, " incredible speed and performance in the home or office, whether you're printing photos, presentations or text. You get phenomenal plain paper print outs. And, EPSON DuraBrite Inks mean the results are both water- and light-resistant", according to its Information Sheet from Epson [1] "Key Features" from the Information sheet include: Optimized for plain paper printing Delivers exceptional results for both photos and everyday printing Water- and light-resistant DuraBriteTM Inks Light resistance up to 80 years on specialty papers and 70 years on plain paper* Cost-saving individual ink cartridges 5760 x 1440 optimized dpi using Resolution Performance ManagementTM (RPMTM) Optimizes the placement of six ink droplet sizes at resolutions up to 5760 x 1440 dpi on various papers for the best detail Up to 22 ppm black text and 11 ppm color printing** Network and wireless options 802.11b Wireless Print Server - Bluetooth Print Adapter from Epson EpsonNet 10/100Base-TX Type B Ethernet Print Server" [1] The printer came with software drivers which worked with Windows® USB (98, 2000, Me and XP),Windows Parallel (95, 98, 2000, NT 4.0, Me and XP), and Macintosh® USB (8.5.1 - 9.x, OS X 10.1 or later). The printer accommodated several standard, pre-set paper sizes, and also allowed user-defined paper sizes from 3.5" x 3.5" up to 8.5" x 44". Reference: Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer Information Sheet, http://www.epson.com/cmc_upload/0/00/013/482/SC82_InfoSheet.pdf. Retrieved 27 June 2013.

title: Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
Epson Stylus C82 Ink Jet Printer
Epson Stylus 740 Color Inkjet Printer
description: Inkjet printers print digital images by propelling ink droplets of varying sizes onto a page. These are currently the most common form of printer. Inkjet technology was first developed in the 1950s, although they weren't mass-produced until the 1970s. Epson is one of the top four main injet printer producers. This printer works with Windows 95/98, Apple MacOS 7.0, or Microsoft Windows 4.0 or later. References: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inkjet_printer http://www.epson.com/cgi-bin/Store/support/supDetail.jsp?

title: Quill Pen
description: For centuries, quill pens represented the finest of writing implements. This re-creation comes from London's historic St. Paul's Cathedral, designed by Sir Christopher Wren.

title: Zenith Data Systems ZWL-0326-08 Laptop Computer
description: Zenith Data Systems (ZDS) was a division of Zenith founded in 1979. This laptop was used by Marna Montgomery to write minutes of school board meetings in 1998 and 1999 and was given to the college on June 11, 2007. The ZWL-0326-08 laptop contains an 80386SL microprocessor. It was built with a miniature 2.5-inch hard disk drive and contains 2M of memory. This laptop operates under either AC or battery power, and it provides approximately three hours of battery-powered computing with each rechargeable battery pack. The laptop offers all the functions and features of a full-size keyboard. The Zenith Data Systems laptop reads data from 3.5-inch 1.4MB and 720K floppy disks on the right of the cabinet, but the machine does not include a built-in mouse; rather an external mouse is connected through a socket on the right (next to the disk drive). It outputs video information to either the built-in 640 x 480 pixel LCD display, or a VGA-type external video monitor. This laptop also provides connectors for serial and parallel peripherals. It supports a standard serial mouse and provides an auxiliary connector for an external keyboard or pointing device. During the late 1980s, Zenith Data Systems was the largest maker of laptops, partnering with Japanese suppliers to fill a contract for 200,000 laptops for the United States Air Force. Reference: ZDS User's Guide.

title: Proxima PROJECTIONLINK PL-300 and PL-300E User & Installation Guide.
description: ProjectionLink is Proxima's revolutionary new solution that simplifies projector installations and connectivity. It's the first single-wire solution that connects your computer and video sources with an installed projector. ProjectionLink includes two components: a transmitter and a receiver. The receiver is usually positioned in the ceiling and connected to the projector. The transmitter allows you to connect multiple input sources to your projector via CAT-5 cabling. This user and installation guide provides more information about ProjectionLink, its installation and configuration. Reference: Proxima User and Installation Guide.

title: Disk Storage Box
description: This box serves as a convenient place to store disks of all sizes. Storing disks prevent them from getting lost thereby keeping them for future use.

title: User's Guide for COMPAQ: Introducing Microsoft Windows 95
description: Manual accompanying purchase of a Microsoft Windows 95 operating system. Gives basic information on the parameters and abilities of the operating system, as well as how to use the general features.
